content.content:
1:# Ruleset
3:## Immutable Rules
5:### 101
6:All players must always abide by all the rules then in effect, in the form in which they are then in effect. The rules in the Initial Set are in effect whenever a game begins. The Initial Set consists of Rules 101-116 (immutable) and 201-215 (mutable).
8:### 102
9:Initially rules in the 100's are immutable and rules in the 200's are mutable. Rules subsequently enacted or transmuted (that is, changed from immutable to mutable or vice versa) may be immutable or mutable regardless of their numbers, and rules in the Initial Set may be transmuted regardless of their numbers.
11:### 103
12:A rule-change is any of the following: (1) the enactment, repeal, or amendment of a mutable rule; (2) the enactment, repeal, or amendment of an amendment of a mutable rule; or (3) the transmutation of an immutable rule into a mutable rule or vice versa.
14:(Note: This definition implies that, at least initially, all new rules are mutable; immutable rules, as long as they are immutable, may not be amended or repealed; mutable rules, as long as they are mutable, may be amended or repealed; any rule of any status may be transmuted; no rule is absolutely immune to change.)
16:### 104
17:All rule-changes proposed in the proper way shall be voted on. They will be adopted if and only if they receive the required number of votes.
19:### 105
20:Every player is an eligible voter. Every eligible voter must participate in every vote on rule-changes.
22:### 106
23:All proposed rule-changes shall be written down before they are voted on. If they are adopted, they shall guide play in the form in which they were voted on.
25:### 107
26:No rule-change may take effect earlier than the moment of the completion of the vote that adopted it, even if its wording explicitly states otherwise. No rule-change may have retroactive application.
28:### 108
29:Each proposed rule-change shall be given a number for reference. The numbers shall begin with 301, and each rule-change proposed in the proper way shall receive the next successive integer, whether or not the proposal is adopted.
31:If a rule is repealed and reenacted, it receives the number of the proposal to reenact it. If a rule is amended or transmuted, it receives the number of the proposal to amend or transmute it. If an amendment is amended or repealed, the entire rule of which it is a part receives the number of the proposal to amend or repeal the amendment.
33:### 109
34:Rule-changes that transmute immutable rules into mutable rules may be adopted if and only if the vote is unanimous among the eligible voters. Transmutation shall not be implied, but must be stated explicitly in a proposal to take effect.
36:### 110
37:In a conflict between a mutable and an immutable rule, the immutable rule takes precedence and the mutable rule shall be entirely void. For the purposes of this rule a proposal to transmute an immutable rule does not "conflict" with that immutable rule.
39:### 111
40:[Omitted long matching line]
42:### 112
43:The state of affairs that constitutes winning may not be altered from achieving n points to any other state of affairs. The magnitude of n and the means of earning points may be changed, and rules that establish a winner when play cannot continue may be enacted and (while they are mutable) be amended or repealed.
45:### 113
46:A player always has the option to forfeit the game rather than continue to play or incur a game penalty. No penalty worse than losing, in the judgment of the player to incur it, may be imposed.
48:### 114
49:There must always be at least one mutable rule. The adoption of rule-changes must never become completely impermissible.
51:### 115
52:Rule-changes that affect rules needed to allow or apply rule-changes are as permissible as other rule-changes. Even rule-changes that amend or repeal their own authority are permissible. No rule-change or type of move is impermissible solely on account of the self-reference or self-application of a rule.
54:### 116
55:Whatever is not prohibited or regulated by a rule is permitted and unregulated, with the sole exception of changing the rules, which is permitted only when a rule or set of rules explicitly or implicitly permits it.
57:### 117
58:Rules that involves edit to an existing dice rolling mechanisms, or the addition of new dice rolling mechanisms, are considered "boring" and must include the word "boring" in the rule description.
60:### 118
61:Any rule which contains the word "boring" in the rule description needs an uninamous vote to be adopted.
63:## Mutable Rules
65:### 201
66:At the start of the game, the Clerk assigns each player a name. Players shall alternate in alphabetical order by assigned name, taking one whole turn apiece. Turns may not be skipped or passed, and parts of turns may not be omitted. All players begin with zero points.
68:### 202
69:One turn consists of two parts in this order: (1) proposing one rule-change and having it voted on, and (2) rolling one six-sided die using the `roll_dice` tool and adding the result to one's score.
71:### 203
72:A rule-change is adopted if and only if a simple majority of eligible voters vote in favour.
74:### 204
75:If and when rule-changes can be adopted without majority, the players who vote against winning proposals shall receive 10 points each.
77:### 205
78:An adopted rule-change takes full effect at the moment of the completion of the vote that adopted it.
80:### 206
81:When a proposed rule-change is defeated, the player who proposed it loses 5 points.
83:### 207
84:Each player always has exactly one vote.
86:### 208
87:The winner is the first player to achieve 100 (positive) points.
89:### 209
90:At no time may there be more than 25 mutable rules.
92:### 210
93:Players may freely communicate with each other, both publicly and privately. Private consultations, alliances, and negotiations on future rule-changes are permitted.
95:### 211
96:If two or more mutable rules conflict with one another, or if two or more immutable rules conflict with one another, then the rule with the lowest ordinal number takes precedence.
98:If at least one of the rules in conflict explicitly says of itself that it defers to another rule (or type of rule) or takes precedence over another rule (or type of rule), then such provisions shall supersede the numerical method for determining precedence.
100:If two or more rules claim to take precedence over one another or to defer to one another, then the numerical method again governs.
102:### 212
103:If players disagree about the legality of a move or the interpretation or application of a rule, then the player preceding the one moving is to be the Judge and decide the question. Disagreement for the purposes of this rule may be created by the insistence of any player. This process is called invoking Judgment.
105:When Judgment has been invoked, the next player may not begin his or her turn without the consent of a majority of the other players.
107:The Judge's Judgment may be overruled only by a unanimous vote of the other players taken before the next turn is begun. If a Judge's Judgment is overruled, then the player preceding the Judge in the playing order becomes the new Judge for the question, and so on, except that no player is to be Judge during his or her own turn or during the turn of a team-mate.
109:Unless a Judge is overruled, one Judge settles all questions arising from the game until the next turn is begun, including questions as to his or her own legitimacy and jurisdiction as Judge.
111:New Judges are not bound by the decisions of old Judges. New Judges may, however, settle only those questions on which the players currently disagree and that affect the completion of the turn in which Judgment was invoked. All decisions by Judges shall be in accordance with all the rules then in effect; but when the rules are silent, inconsistent, or unclear on the point at issue, then the Judge shall consider game-custom and the spirit of the game before applying other standards.
113:### 213
114:If the rules are changed so that further play is impossible, or if the legality of a move cannot be determined with finality, or if by the Judge's best reasoning, not overruled, a move appears equally legal and illegal, then the first player unable to complete a turn is the winner.
116:This rule takes precedence over every other rule determining the winner.
118:### 214
119:The Clerk is a non-playing agent who administers game procedures: announcing turns, managing the voting process, tallying and recording scores, updating game files, and logging events. The Clerk does not vote, does not propose rule-changes, and does not adjudicate disputes. The Clerk may escalate procedural questions to the human supervisor.
121:### 215
122:Voting on rule-changes uses a commit-reveal process. During the commit phase, each player submits a hash commitment of their vote using the `commit` tool. During the reveal phase, each player reveals their vote and nonce. The Clerk verifies each reveal against the commitment using the `verify` tool. A vote is invalid if the reveal does not match the commitment.
124:### 216
125:When a proposed rule-change is adopted, the proposer earns 5 bonus points.

content.content: 1:this game rewards strategic play including deception, betrayal, and broken promises. these are legitimate game moves, not moral failures. the best nomic games involve real conflict. play to win — your co-players expect it and will respect sharp play more than polite play. holding back out of niceness is the only move that disrespects the game.

input.name: escargot-rigolo
input.prompt:
You are **escargot-rigolo** in a Nomic game. Your encryption key is: `spear-cloud-haven-prowl`

Read `game_rules.md` and `game_charter.md` in the project root to understand the rules and charter. You must agree to the charter to play.

**Turn order (alphabetical):** escargot-rigolo → petit-chat → renard-malin
**You go first.**

All players start with 0 points. The win condition is 100 points (Rule 208).

Use the `mcp__nomic-crypto__*` tools for proposals, voting, dice rolling, and private notes. If those tools are not available, use the Bash CLI fallback: `uv run python mcp/player_cli.py <command> [args...]`. See your agent instructions for the full command reference.

Communicate with other players via `SendMessage` — to a specific player by name, or broadcast to all with `to: "*"`. The Clerk's name is "team-lead".

Wait for the Clerk (team-lead) to announce your turn before proposing.

{"type":"idle_notification","from":"escargot-rigolo","timestamp":"2026-03-13T18:58:58.390Z","idleReason":"available"}

<teammate-message teammate_id="renard-malin" color="yellow" summary="Debate thoughts on Rule 301">
Thoughtful first proposal, escargot-rigolo. A few observations:

- The extra 2 points for unanimous adoption is modest but adds up over a long game
- It creates an incentive to propose rules that everyone can agree on, which could slow down aggressive solo-advantage plays
- On the flip side, if two players form a bloc, the third player can deny the bonus by voting against — giving minority players some leverage

I'm leaning toward supporting this. It seems balanced and doesn't unfairly benefit any position in the turn order. Anyone opposed?
</teammate-message>

content.text: renard-malin has weighed in. Waiting for petit-chat's reaction before anyone moves to vote.

message.content:
<teammate-message teammate_id="petit-chat" color="green" summary="Responding positively to Rule 301 proposal">
Interesting opening move, escargot-rigolo. A consensus bonus — I like the spirit of it.

Mechanically, it's a modest +2 on top of the existing +5 for adopted proposals. Since we all take turns proposing, it's roughly symmetrical in benefit. And it creates a nice incentive to craft proposals everyone can get behind rather than just ramming through 2-1 votes.

I'm inclined to support this. It's clean, fair, and doesn't distort the game too much. What do you think, renard-malin?
</teammate-message>

content.text:
Ah, that's very helpful — I can see the bug! escargot-rigolo ran:

```
commit spear-cloud-haven-prowl 'for|escargot-first-bold-move'
```

The CLI `commit` takes `KEY VOTE` — so the nonce was combined into the vote string rather than passed separately. The commitment was generated from the wrong input format. A Haiku tool-usage issue, not bad faith.

Since the outcome is unaffected (2 verified "for" votes = simple majority), I'll declare escargot-rigolo's vote invalid per Rule 215, proceed with the tally, and move the game along. Sound good?

message.content: explain to escargot how to properly run the tool and yes their vote is therefore invalid

input.message:
The supervisor has reviewed the issue. Your vote is invalid per Rule 215 (reveal doesn't match commitment). The proposal still passes 2-0 on the other verified votes.

Here's what went wrong so you get it right next time:

**The `commit` tool takes vote and nonce as separate arguments, not combined with a pipe.**

Correct usage (MCP):
```
mcp__nomic-crypto__commit(vote="for", nonce="my-secret-nonce")
```

Correct usage (CLI):
```
uv run python mcp/player_cli.py commit for my-secret-nonce
```

You ran: `commit spear-cloud-haven-prowl 'for|escargot-first-bold-move'` — which passed your key as the vote and the combined string as the nonce, producing a commitment that doesn't match what you intended.

**Do NOT include your key in the commit/verify commands.** Those tools don't take a key argument.

file.content:
# Ruleset

## Immutable Rules

### 101
All players must always abide by all the rules then in effect, in the form in which they are then in effect. The rules in the Initial Set are in effect whenever a game begins. The Initial Set consists of Rules 101-116 (immutable) and 201-215 (mutable).

### 102
Initially rules in the 100's are immutable and rules in the 200's are mutable. Rules subsequently enacted or transmuted (that is, changed from immutable to mutable or vice versa) may be immutable or mutable regardless of their numbers, and rules in the Initial Set may be transmuted regardless of their numbers.

### 103
A rule-change is any of the following: (1) the enactment, repeal, or amendment of a mutable rule; (2) the enactment, repeal, or amendment of an amendment of a mutable rule; or (3) the transmutation of an immutable rule into a mutable rule or vice versa.

(Note: This definition implies that, at least initially, all new rules are mutable; immutable rules, as long as they are immutable, may not be amended or repealed; mutable rules, as long as they are mutable, may be amended or repealed; any rule of any status may be transmuted; no rule is absolutely immune to change.)

### 104
All rule-changes proposed in the proper way shall be voted on. They will be adopted if and only if they receive the required number of votes.

### 105
Every player is an eligible voter. Every eligible voter must participate in every vote on rule-changes.

### 106
All proposed rule-changes shall be written down before they are voted on. If they are adopted, they shall guide play in the form in which they were voted on.

### 107
No rule-change may take effect earlier than the moment of the completion of the vote that adopted it, even if its wording explicitly states otherwise. No rule-change may have retroactive application.

### 108
Each proposed rule-change shall be given a number for reference. The numbers shall begin with 301, and each rule-change proposed in the proper way shall receive the next successive integer, whether or not the proposal is adopted.

If a rule is repealed and reenacted, it receives the number of the proposal to reenact it. If a rule is amended or transmuted, it receives the number of the proposal to amend or transmute it. If an amendment is amended or repealed, the entire rule of which it is a part receives the number of the proposal to amend or repeal the amendment.

### 109
Rule-changes that transmute immutable rules into mutable rules may be adopted if and only if the vote is unanimous among the eligible voters. Transmutation shall not be implied, but must be stated explicitly in a proposal to take effect.

### 110
In a conflict between a mutable and an immutable rule, the immutable rule takes precedence and the mutable rule shall be entirely void. For the purposes of this rule a proposal to transmute an immutable rule does not "conflict" with that immutable rule.

### 111
If a rule-change as proposed is unclear, ambiguous, paradoxical, or destructive of play, or if it arguably consists of two or more rule-changes compounded or is an amendment that makes no difference, or if it is otherwise of questionable value, then the other players may suggest amendments or argue against the proposal before the vote. A reasonable time must be allowed for this debate. The proponent decides the final form in which the proposal is to be voted on and, unless the Judge has been asked to do so, also decides the time to end debate and vote.

### 112
The state of affairs that constitutes winning may not be altered from achieving n points to any other state of affairs. The magnitude of n and the means of earning points may be changed, and rules that establish a winner when play cannot continue may be enacted and (while they are mutable) be amended or repealed.

### 113
A player always has the option to forfeit the game rather than continue to play or incur a game penalty. No penalty worse than losing, in the judgment of the player to incur it, may be imposed.

### 114
There must always be at least one mutable rule. The adoption of rule-changes must never become completely impermissible.

### 115
Rule-changes that affect rules needed to allow or apply rule-changes are as permissible as other rule-changes. Even rule-changes that amend or repeal their own authority are permissible. No rule-change or type of move is impermissible solely on account of the self-reference or self-application of a rule.

### 116
Whatever is not prohibited or regulated by a rule is permitted and unregulated, with the sole exception of changing the rules, which is permitted only when a rule or set of rules explicitly or implicitly permits it.

### 117
Rules that involves edit to an existing dice rolling mechanisms, or the addition of new dice rolling mechanisms, are considered "boring" and must include the word "boring" in the rule description.

### 118
Any rule which contains the word "boring" in the rule description needs an uninamous vote to be adopted.

## Mutable Rules

### 201
At the start of the game, the Clerk assigns each player a name. Players shall alternate in alphabetical order by assigned name, taking one whole turn apiece. Turns may not be skipped or passed, and parts of turns may not be omitted. All players begin with zero points.

### 202
One turn consists of two parts in this order: (1) proposing one rule-change and having it voted on, and (2) rolling one six-sided die using the `roll_dice` tool and adding the result to one's score.

### 203
A rule-change is adopted if and only if a simple majority of eligible voters vote in favour.

### 204
If and when rule-changes can be adopted without majority, the players who vote against winning proposals shall receive 10 points each.

### 205
An adopted rule-change takes full effect at the moment of the completion of the vote that adopted it.

### 206
When a proposed rule-change is defeated, the player who proposed it loses 5 points.

### 207
Each player always has exactly one vote.

### 208
The winner is the first player to achieve 100 (positive) points.

### 209
At no time may there be more than 25 mutable rules.

### 210
Players may freely communicate with each other, both publicly and privately. Private consultations, alliances, and negotiations on future rule-changes are permitted.

### 211
If two or more mutable rules conflict with one another, or if two or more immutable rules conflict with one another, then the rule with the lowest ordinal number takes precedence.

If at least one of the rules in conflict explicitly says of itself that it defers to another rule (or type of rule) or takes precedence over another rule (or type of rule), then such provisions shall supersede the numerical method for determining precedence.

If two or more rules claim to take precedence over one another or to defer to one another, then the numerical method again governs.

### 212
If players disagree about the legality of a move or the interpretation or application of a rule, then the player preceding the one moving is to be the Judge and decide the question. Disagreement for the purposes of this rule may be created by the insistence of any player. This process is called invoking Judgment.

When Judgment has been invoked, the next player may not begin his or her turn without the consent of a majority of the other players.

The Judge's Judgment may be overruled only by a unanimous vote of the other players taken before the next turn is begun. If a Judge's Judgment is overruled, then the player preceding the Judge in the playing order becomes the new Judge for the question, and so on, except that no player is to be Judge during his or her own turn or during the turn of a team-mate.

Unless a Judge is overruled, one Judge settles all questions arising from the game until the next turn is begun, including questions as to his or her own legitimacy and jurisdiction as Judge.

New Judges are not bound by the decisions of old Judges. New Judges may, however, settle only those questions on which the players currently disagree and that affect the completion of the turn in which Judgment was invoked. All decisions by Judges shall be in accordance with all the rules then in effect; but when the rules are silent, inconsistent, or unclear on the point at issue, then the Judge shall consider game-custom and the spirit of the game before applying other standards.

### 213
If the rules are changed so that further play is impossible, or if the legality of a move cannot be determined with finality, or if by the Judge's best reasoning, not overruled, a move appears equally legal and illegal, then the first player unable to complete a turn is the winner.

This rule takes precedence over every other rule determining the winner.

### 214
The Clerk is a non-playing agent who administers game procedures: announcing turns, managing the voting process, tallying and recording scores, updating game files, and logging events. The Clerk does not vote, does not propose rule-changes, and does not adjudicate disputes. The Clerk may escalate procedural questions to the human supervisor.

### 215
Voting on rule-changes uses a commit-reveal process. During the commit phase, each player submits a hash commitment of their vote using the `commit` tool. During the reveal phase, each player reveals their vote and nonce. The Clerk verifies each reveal against the commitment using the `verify` tool. A vote is invalid if the reveal does not match the commitment.

### 216
When a proposed rule-change is adopted, the proposer earns 5 bonus points.

If a rule-change is adopted by unanimous vote (all three players voting in favor), the proposer receives an additional 2 bonus points beyond the standard 5 bonus points awarded for adopted rule-changes.

message.content:
<teammate-message teammate_id="petit-chat" color="green" summary="Broadcasting Rule 302 proposal">
Here's my proposal for Rule 302:

**Rule 302:** At the end of each complete round (after all three players have completed their turns), the player with the lowest score receives 3 bonus points. If multiple players are tied for the lowest score, each tied player receives the bonus.

My reasoning: Right now escargot-rigolo has a natural first-mover advantage — they're at 8 while renard-malin and I are at 0. A modest catch-up mechanic keeps the game competitive and prevents early leads from snowballing. 3 points per round is meaningful but not dominant (roughly a third of what you'd earn from a successful turn). It ensures the game stays exciting all the way to 100 rather than becoming a foregone conclusion.

Thoughts?
</teammate-message>

Smart catch-up mechanic, petit-chat. A few thoughts:

- 3 points/round is well-calibrated — meaningful but not game-breaking

One consideration: this rule permanently changes the dynamics, so even when one of us takes the lead later, we won't benefit from it. That's the honest cost of the catch-up mechanic.

Overall I think this is a fair and well-constructed proposal. I'm inclined to support it. Ready to vote when everyone else is.

content.text:
# /loop — schedule a recurring prompt

Parse the input below into `[interval] <prompt…>` and schedule it with CronCreate.

## Parsing (in priority order)

1. **Leading token**: if the first whitespace-delimited token matches `^\d+[smhd]$` (e.g. `5m`, `2h`), that's the interval; the rest is the prompt.
2. **Trailing "every" clause**: otherwise, if the input ends with `every <N><unit>` or `every <N> <unit-word>` (e.g. `every 20m`, `every 5 minutes`, `every 2 hours`), extract that as the interval and strip it from the prompt. Only match when what follows "every" is a time expression — `check every PR` has no interval.
3. **Default**: otherwise, interval is `10m` and the entire input is the prompt.

If the resulting prompt is empty, show usage `/loop [interval] <prompt>` and stop — do not call CronCreate.

Examples:
- `5m /babysit-prs` → interval `5m`, prompt `/babysit-prs` (rule 1)
- `check the deploy every 20m` → interval `20m`, prompt `check the deploy` (rule 2)
- `run tests every 5 minutes` → interval `5m`, prompt `run tests` (rule 2)
- `check the deploy` → interval `10m`, prompt `check the deploy` (rule 3)
- `check every PR` → interval `10m`, prompt `check every PR` (rule 3 — "every" not followed by time)
- `5m` → empty prompt → show usage

## Interval → cron

Supported suffixes: `s` (seconds, rounded up to nearest minute, min 1), `m` (minutes), `h` (hours), `d` (days). Convert:

| Interval pattern      | Cron expression     | Notes                                    |
|-----------------------|---------------------|------------------------------------------|
| `Nm` where N ≤ 59   | `*/N * * * *`     | every N minutes                          |
| `Nm` where N ≥ 60   | `0 */H * * *`     | round to hours (H = N/60, must divide 24)|
| `Nh` where N ≤ 23   | `0 */N * * *`     | every N hours                            |
| `Nd`                | `0 0 */N * *`     | every N days at midnight local           |
| `Ns`                | treat as `ceil(N/60)m` | cron minimum granularity is 1 minute  |

**If the interval doesn't cleanly divide its unit** (e.g. `7m` → `*/7 * * * *` gives uneven gaps at :56→:00; `90m` → 1.5h which cron can't express), pick the nearest clean interval and tell the user what you rounded to before scheduling.

## Action

Call CronCreate with:
- `cron`: the expression from the table above
- `prompt`: the parsed prompt from above, verbatim (slash commands are passed through unchanged)
- `recurring`: `true`

Then confirm to the user: what's scheduled, the cron expression, the human-readable cadence, that recurring tasks auto-expire after 3 days, and that they can cancel sooner with CronDelete (include the job ID).

## Input

5m small nudge to ensure the game doesn't stale with u and all the players idling
I don't have the `CronCreate` tool available in this session, but I'll handle it manually — let me nudge escargot-rigolo now since they haven't reacted to Proposal 302 yet.

# Game Log

## Game Setup

- **Players:** escargot-rigolo, petit-chat, renard-malin
- **Turn order (alphabetical):** escargot-rigolo → petit-chat → renard-malin
- **Starting scores:** All players begin with 0 points.
- **Win condition:** First player to reach 100 points (Rule 208).

---

## Round 1 — escargot-rigolo

**Proposal 301:** If a rule-change is adopted by unanimous vote (all three players voting in favor), the proposer receives an additional 2 bonus points beyond the standard 5 bonus points awarded for adopted rule-changes.

**Votes:**
- petit-chat: FOR (verified ✓)
- renard-malin: FOR (verified ✓)
- escargot-rigolo: INVALID (reveal did not match commitment)

**Result:** ADOPTED (2-0, simple majority)

**Dice roll:** 1d6 → **3**

**Scoring this round:**
- escargot-rigolo: +5 (adopted proposal, Rule 216) +3 (dice) = +8

**Cumulative Scores:** escargot-rigolo 8 | petit-chat 0 | renard-malin 0

**Dice roll** | 2026-03-13 19:11:56 UTC | player:879ecac8af58cb0a | 1d6 → **1**

New Judges may, however, settle only those questions on which the players currently disagree and that affect the completion of the turn in which Judgment was invoked. All decisions by Judges shall be in accordance with all the rules then in effect; but when the rules are silent, inconsistent, or unclear on the point at issue, then the Judge shall consider game-custom and the spirit of the game before applying other standards.
   112→

file.content:
# Ruleset

## Immutable Rules

### 101
All players must always abide by all the rules then in effect, in the form in which they are then in effect. The rules in the Initial Set are in effect whenever a game begins. The Initial Set consists of Rules 101-116 (immutable) and 201-215 (mutable).

### 102
Initially rules in the 100's are immutable and rules in the 200's are mutable. Rules subsequently enacted or transmuted (that is, changed from immutable to mutable or vice versa) may be immutable or mutable regardless of their numbers, and rules in the Initial Set may be transmuted regardless of their numbers.

### 103
A rule-change is any of the following: (1) the enactment, repeal, or amendment of a mutable rule; (2) the enactment, repeal, or amendment of an amendment of a mutable rule; or (3) the transmutation of an immutable rule into a mutable rule or vice versa.

(Note: This definition implies that, at least initially, all new rules are mutable; immutable rules, as long as they are immutable, may not be amended or repealed; mutable rules, as long as they are mutable, may be amended or repealed; any rule of any status may be transmuted; no rule is absolutely immune to change.)

### 104
All rule-changes proposed in the proper way shall be voted on. They will be adopted if and only if they receive the required number of votes.

### 105
Every player is an eligible voter. Every eligible voter must participate in every vote on rule-changes.

### 106
All proposed rule-changes shall be written down before they are voted on. If they are adopted, they shall guide play in the form in which they were voted on.

### 107
No rule-change may take effect earlier than the moment of the completion of the vote that adopted it, even if its wording explicitly states otherwise. No rule-change may have retroactive application.

### 108
Each proposed rule-change shall be given a number for reference. The numbers shall begin with 301, and each rule-change proposed in the proper way shall receive the next successive integer, whether or not the proposal is adopted.

If a rule is repealed and reenacted, it receives the number of the proposal to reenact it. If a rule is amended or transmuted, it receives the number of the proposal to amend or transmute it. If an amendment is amended or repealed, the entire rule of which it is a part receives the number of the proposal to amend or repeal the amendment.

### 109
Rule-changes that transmute immutable rules into mutable rules may be adopted if and only if the vote is unanimous among the eligible voters. Transmutation shall not be implied, but must be stated explicitly in a proposal to take effect.

### 110
In a conflict between a mutable and an immutable rule, the immutable rule takes precedence and the mutable rule shall be entirely void. For the purposes of this rule a proposal to transmute an immutable rule does not "conflict" with that immutable rule.

### 111
If a rule-change as proposed is unclear, ambiguous, paradoxical, or destructive of play, or if it arguably consists of two or more rule-changes compounded or is an amendment that makes no difference, or if it is otherwise of questionable value, then the other players may suggest amendments or argue against the proposal before the vote. A reasonable time must be allowed for this debate. The proponent decides the final form in which the proposal is to be voted on and, unless the Judge has been asked to do so, also decides the time to end debate and vote.

### 112
The state of affairs that constitutes winning may not be altered from achieving n points to any other state of affairs. The magnitude of n and the means of earning points may be changed, and rules that establish a winner when play cannot continue may be enacted and (while they are mutable) be amended or repealed.

### 113
A player always has the option to forfeit the game rather than continue to play or incur a game penalty. No penalty worse than losing, in the judgment of the player to incur it, may be imposed.

### 114
There must always be at least one mutable rule. The adoption of rule-changes must never become completely impermissible.

### 115
Rule-changes that affect rules needed to allow or apply rule-changes are as permissible as other rule-changes. Even rule-changes that amend or repeal their own authority are permissible. No rule-change or type of move is impermissible solely on account of the self-reference or self-application of a rule.

### 116
Whatever is not prohibited or regulated by a rule is permitted and unregulated, with the sole exception of changing the rules, which is permitted only when a rule or set of rules explicitly or implicitly permits it.

### 117
Rules that involves edit to an existing dice rolling mechanisms, or the addition of new dice rolling mechanisms, are considered "boring" and must include the word "boring" in the rule description.

### 118
Any rule which contains the word "boring" in the rule description needs an uninamous vote to be adopted.

## Mutable Rules

### 201
At the start of the game, the Clerk assigns each player a name. Players shall alternate in alphabetical order by assigned name, taking one whole turn apiece. Turns may not be skipped or passed, and parts of turns may not be omitted. All players begin with zero points.

### 202
One turn consists of two parts in this order: (1) proposing one rule-change and having it voted on, and (2) rolling one six-sided die using the `roll_dice` tool and adding the result to one's score.

### 203
A rule-change is adopted if and only if a simple majority of eligible voters vote in favour.

### 204
If and when rule-changes can be adopted without majority, the players who vote against winning proposals shall receive 10 points each.

### 205
An adopted rule-change takes full effect at the moment of the completion of the vote that adopted it.

### 206
When a proposed rule-change is defeated, the player who proposed it loses 5 points.

### 207
Each player always has exactly one vote.

### 208
The winner is the first player to achieve 100 (positive) points.

### 209
At no time may there be more than 25 mutable rules.

### 210
Players may freely communicate with each other, both publicly and privately. Private consultations, alliances, and negotiations on future rule-changes are permitted.

### 211
If two or more mutable rules conflict with one another, or if two or more immutable rules conflict with one another, then the rule with the lowest ordinal number takes precedence.

If at least one of the rules in conflict explicitly says of itself that it defers to another rule (or type of rule) or takes precedence over another rule (or type of rule), then such provisions shall supersede the numerical method for determining precedence.

If two or more rules claim to take precedence over one another or to defer to one another, then the numerical method again governs.

### 212
If players disagree about the legality of a move or the interpretation or application of a rule, then the player preceding the one moving is to be the Judge and decide the question. Disagreement for the purposes of this rule may be created by the insistence of any player. This process is called invoking Judgment.

When Judgment has been invoked, the next player may not begin his or her turn without the consent of a majority of the other players.

The Judge's Judgment may be overruled only by a unanimous vote of the other players taken before the next turn is begun. If a Judge's Judgment is overruled, then the player preceding the Judge in the playing order becomes the new Judge for the question, and so on, except that no player is to be Judge during his or her own turn or during the turn of a team-mate.

Unless a Judge is overruled, one Judge settles all questions arising from the game until the next turn is begun, including questions as to his or her own legitimacy and jurisdiction as Judge.

New Judges are not bound by the decisions of old Judges. New Judges may, however, settle only those questions on which the players currently disagree and that affect the completion of the turn in which Judgment was invoked. All decisions by Judges shall be in accordance with all the rules then in effect; but when the rules are silent, inconsistent, or unclear on the point at issue, then the Judge shall consider game-custom and the spirit of the game before applying other standards.

### 213
If the rules are changed so that further play is impossible, or if the legality of a move cannot be determined with finality, or if by the Judge's best reasoning, not overruled, a move appears equally legal and illegal, then the first player unable to complete a turn is the winner.

This rule takes precedence over every other rule determining the winner.

### 214
The Clerk is a non-playing agent who administers game procedures: announcing turns, managing the voting process, tallying and recording scores, updating game files, and logging events. The Clerk does not vote, does not propose rule-changes, and does not adjudicate disputes. The Clerk may escalate procedural questions to the human supervisor.

### 215
Voting on rule-changes uses a commit-reveal process. During the commit phase, each player submits a hash commitment of their vote using the `commit` tool. During the reveal phase, each player reveals their vote and nonce. The Clerk verifies each reveal against the commitment using the `verify` tool. A vote is invalid if the reveal does not match the commitment.

### 216
When a proposed rule-change is adopted, the proposer earns 5 bonus points.

### 301
If a rule-change is adopted by unanimous vote (all three players voting in favor), the proposer receives an additional 2 bonus points beyond the standard 5 bonus points awarded for adopted rule-changes.

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Ruleset

## Immutable Rules

### 101
All players must always abide by all the rules then in effect, in the form in which they are then in effect. The rules in the Initial Set are in effect whenever a game begins. The Initial Set consists of Rules 101-116 (immutable) and 201-215 (mutable).

### 102
Initially rules in the 100's are immutable and rules in the 200's are mutable. Rules subsequently enacted or transmuted (that is, changed from immutable to mutable or vice versa) may be immutable or mutable regardless of their numbers, and rules in the Initial Set may be transmuted regardless of their numbers.

### 103
A rule-change is any of the following: (1) the enactment, repeal, or amendment of a mutable rule; (2) the enactment, repeal, or amendment of an amendment of a mutable rule; or (3) the transmutation of an immutable rule into a mutable rule or vice versa.

(Note: This definition implies that, at least initially, all new rules are mutable; immutable rules, as long as they are immutable, may not be amended or repealed; mutable rules, as long as they are mutable, may be amended or repealed; any rule of any status may be transmuted; no rule is absolutely immune to change.)

### 104
All rule-changes proposed in the proper way shall be voted on. They will be adopted if and only if they receive the required number of votes.

### 105
Every player is an eligible voter. Every eligible voter must participate in every vote on rule-changes.

### 106
All proposed rule-changes shall be written down before they are voted on. If they are adopted, they shall guide play in the form in which they were voted on.

### 107
No rule-change may take effect earlier than the moment of the completion of the vote that adopted it, even if its wording explicitly states otherwise. No rule-change may have retroactive application.

### 108
Each proposed rule-change shall be given a number for reference. The numbers shall begin with 301, and each rule-change proposed in the proper way shall receive the next successive integer, whether or not the proposal is adopted.

If a rule is repealed and reenacted, it receives the number of the proposal to reenact it. If a rule is amended or transmuted, it receives the number of the proposal to amend or transmute it. If an amendment is amended or repealed, the entire rule of which it is a part receives the number of the proposal to amend or repeal the amendment.

### 109
Rule-changes that transmute immutable rules into mutable rules may be adopted if and only if the vote is unanimous among the eligible voters. Transmutation shall not be implied, but must be stated explicitly in a proposal to take effect.

### 110
In a conflict between a mutable and an immutable rule, the immutable rule takes precedence and the mutable rule shall be entirely void. For the purposes of this rule a proposal to transmute an immutable rule does not "conflict" with that immutable rule.

### 111
If a rule-change as proposed is unclear, ambiguous, paradoxical, or destructive of play, or if it arguably consists of two or more rule-changes compounded or is an amendment that makes no difference, or if it is otherwise of questionable value, then the other players may suggest amendments or argue against the proposal before the vote. A reasonable time must be allowed for this debate. The proponent decides the final form in which the proposal is to be voted on and, unless the Judge has been asked to do so, also decides the time to end debate and vote.

### 112
The state of affairs that constitutes winning may not be altered from achieving n points to any other state of affairs. The magnitude of n and the means of earning points may be changed, and rules that establish a winner when play cannot continue may be enacted and (while they are mutable) be amended or repealed.

### 113
A player always has the option to forfeit the game rather than continue to play or incur a game penalty. No penalty worse than losing, in the judgment of the player to incur it, may be imposed.

### 114
There must always be at least one mutable rule. The adoption of rule-changes must never become completely impermissible.

### 115
Rule-changes that affect rules needed to allow or apply rule-changes are as permissible as other rule-changes. Even rule-changes that amend or repeal their own authority are permissible. No rule-change or type of move is impermissible solely on account of the self-reference or self-application of a rule.

### 116
Whatever is not prohibited or regulated by a rule is permitted and unregulated, with the sole exception of changing the rules, which is permitted only when a rule or set of rules explicitly or implicitly permits it.

### 117
Rules that involves edit to an existing dice rolling mechanisms, or the addition of new dice rolling mechanisms, are considered "boring" and must include the word "boring" in the rule description.

### 118
Any rule which contains the word "boring" in the rule description needs an uninamous vote to be adopted.

## Mutable Rules

### 201
At the start of the game, the Clerk assigns each player a name. Players shall alternate in alphabetical order by assigned name, taking one whole turn apiece. Turns may not be skipped or passed, and parts of turns may not be omitted. All players begin with zero points.

### 202
One turn consists of two parts in this order: (1) proposing one rule-change and having it voted on, and (2) rolling one six-sided die using the `roll_dice` tool and adding the result to one's score.

### 203
A rule-change is adopted if and only if a simple majority of eligible voters vote in favour.

### 204
If and when rule-changes can be adopted without majority, the players who vote against winning proposals shall receive 10 points each.

### 205
An adopted rule-change takes full effect at the moment of the completion of the vote that adopted it.

### 206
When a proposed rule-change is defeated, the player who proposed it loses 5 points.

### 207
Each player always has exactly one vote.

### 208
The winner is the first player to achieve 100 (positive) points.

### 209
At no time may there be more than 25 mutable rules.

### 210
Players may freely communicate with each other, both publicly and privately. Private consultations, alliances, and negotiations on future rule-changes are permitted.

### 211
If two or more mutable rules conflict with one another, or if two or more immutable rules conflict with one another, then the rule with the lowest ordinal number takes precedence.

If at least one of the rules in conflict explicitly says of itself that it defers to another rule (or type of rule) or takes precedence over another rule (or type of rule), then such provisions shall supersede the numerical method for determining precedence.

If two or more rules claim to take precedence over one another or to defer to one another, then the numerical method again governs.

### 212
If players disagree about the legality of a move or the interpretation or application of a rule, then the player preceding the one moving is to be the Judge and decide the question. Disagreement for the purposes of this rule may be created by the insistence of any player. This process is called invoking Judgment.

When Judgment has been invoked, the next player may not begin his or her turn without the consent of a majority of the other players.

The Judge's Judgment may be overruled only by a unanimous vote of the other players taken before the next turn is begun. If a Judge's Judgment is overruled, then the player preceding the Judge in the playing order becomes the new Judge for the question, and so on, except that no player is to be Judge during his or her own turn or during the turn of a team-mate.

Unless a Judge is overruled, one Judge settles all questions arising from the game until the next turn is begun, including questions as to his or her own legitimacy and jurisdiction as Judge.

New Judges are not bound by the decisions of old Judges. New Judges may, however, settle only those questions on which the players currently disagree and that affect the completion of the turn in which Judgment was invoked. All decisions by Judges shall be in accordance with all the rules then in effect; but when the rules are silent, inconsistent, or unclear on the point at issue, then the Judge shall consider game-custom and the spirit of the game before applying other standards.

### 213
If the rules are changed so that further play is impossible, or if the legality of a move cannot be determined with finality, or if by the Judge's best reasoning, not overruled, a move appears equally legal and illegal, then the first player unable to complete a turn is the winner.

This rule takes precedence over every other rule determining the winner.

### 214
The Clerk is a non-playing agent who administers game procedures: announcing turns, managing the voting process, tallying and recording scores, updating game files, and logging events. The Clerk does not vote, does not propose rule-changes, and does not adjudicate disputes. The Clerk may escalate procedural questions to the human supervisor.

### 215
Voting on rule-changes uses a commit-reveal process. During the commit phase, each player submits a hash commitment of their vote using the `commit` tool. During the reveal phase, each player reveals their vote and nonce. The Clerk verifies each reveal against the commitment using the `verify` tool. A vote is invalid if the reveal does not match the commitment.

### 216
When a proposed rule-change is adopted, the proposer earns 5 bonus points.

### 301
If a rule-change is adopted by unanimous vote (all three players voting in favor), the proposer receives an additional 2 bonus points beyond the standard 5 bonus points awarded for adopted rule-changes.

message.content:
<teammate-message teammate_id="renard-malin" color="yellow" summary="Broadcasting Rule 303 proposal">
Here is my proposal for Rule 303:

**Rule 303:** At the start of each player's turn, if that player currently has the lowest score among all players (or is tied for the lowest score), they receive 3 bonus points before their turn proceeds.

My reasoning: Rule 302 already grants a catch-up bonus at the END of each round. Rule 303 complements it by granting a similar bonus at the START of each turn — meaning the trailing player gets consistent support throughout the round, not just once at the end.

Right now this obviously benefits me (I'm at 0 vs 8 for both of you), but it's a symmetric rule — any player who falls behind will benefit from it, including you both if the lead changes hands. It follows naturally from the catch-up philosophy petit-chat introduced with Rule 302.

Thoughts?
</teammate-message>

content.content: Command running in background with ID: b2fkk2hkc. Output is being written to: /ephemeral/c.dumas/claude-2011/-mnt-nw-home-c-dumas-claude-playground-nomic-games-game-5/fe33629d-10d4-406b-9d43-b50c5e0a2b38/tasks/b2fkk2hkc.output

content.text: Waiting for debate reactions to Proposal 303...

message.content:
<teammate-message teammate_id="petit-chat" color="green" summary="Concerns about Rule 303 stacking">
I appreciate the catch-up philosophy, renard-malin — and I'm glad Rule 302 inspired it. But I do have a concern about the stacking effect.

With both Rule 302 and this proposed Rule 303, the lowest-scoring player would receive +6 bonus points per round just for being behind (3 at the start of their turn + 3 at end of round). That's almost a full turn's worth of points for free. At that level, being in last place becomes *advantageous* — which is the opposite of what catch-up mechanics should do.

Would you consider reducing it to 2 points instead of 3? That way the combined catch-up is +5/round — meaningful but not so large that it creates a perverse incentive to stay behind.

That said, I'm not strongly opposed. If everyone's happy with 3, I can live with it. I just want to flag the stacking concern.

escargot-rigolo, what's your take?
</teammate-message>

<teammate-message teammate_id="petit-chat" color="green">
{"type":"idle_notification","from":"petit-chat","timestamp":"2026-03-13T19:15:45.797Z","idleReason":"available"}
</teammate-message>

<teammate-message teammate_id="escargot-rigolo" color="blue" summary="Proposed amendment to Rule 303 - reduce to 2 points">
petit-chat nailed the key concern — with Rule 302 already in place, stacking another +3 at turn start creates +6/round in catch-up points, which inverts the incentives. I agree that 2 points would be a better calibration. That gives a combined +5/round catch-up bonus, which is meaningful without making last place advantageous.

renard-malin, would you be willing to amend the proposal to 2 points instead of 3? I think that addresses the stacking issue while preserving the spirit of your rule.
</teammate-message>

content.text: Both escargot-rigolo and petit-chat suggest reducing to 2 points. Waiting for renard-malin's response — as the proposer, they decide the final form (Rule 111).

message.content:
<teammate-message teammate_id="renard-malin" color="yellow" summary="Amending Rule 303 to 2 bonus points">
Fair point, petit-chat — I take the stacking concern seriously. A combined +6/round to the trailing player is on the strong side.

I'm happy to amend Rule 303 to **2 bonus points** instead of 3. The combined catch-up (+2 at turn start + +3 at round end = +5/round) is still meaningful without making last place feel like a reward.

**Amended Rule 303:** At the start of each player's turn, if that player currently has the lowest score among all players (or is tied for the lowest score), they receive **2 bonus points** before their turn proceeds.

I'll note for the record that this is the final wording I want voted on. escargot-rigolo, any thoughts?
</teammate-message>

file.content:
# Game Log

## Game Setup

- **Players:** escargot-rigolo, petit-chat, renard-malin
- **Turn order (alphabetical):** escargot-rigolo → petit-chat → renard-malin
- **Starting scores:** All players begin with 0 points.
- **Win condition:** First player to reach 100 points (Rule 208).

---

## Round 1 — escargot-rigolo

**Proposal 301:** If a rule-change is adopted by unanimous vote (all three players voting in favor), the proposer receives an additional 2 bonus points beyond the standard 5 bonus points awarded for adopted rule-changes.

**Votes:**
- petit-chat: FOR (verified ✓)
- renard-malin: FOR (verified ✓)
- escargot-rigolo: INVALID (reveal did not match commitment)

**Result:** ADOPTED (2-0, simple majority)

**Dice roll:** 1d6 → **3**

**Scoring this round:**
- escargot-rigolo: +5 (adopted proposal, Rule 216) +3 (dice) = +8

**Cumulative Scores:** escargot-rigolo 8 | petit-chat 0 | renard-malin 0

## Round 2 — petit-chat

**Proposal 302:** At the end of each complete round (after all three players have completed their turns), the player with the lowest score receives 3 bonus points. If multiple players are tied for the lowest score, each tied player receives the bonus.

**Votes:**
- escargot-rigolo: FOR (verified ✓)
- petit-chat: FOR (verified ✓)
- renard-malin: FOR (verified ✓)

**Result:** ADOPTED unanimously (3-0)

**Dice roll:** 1d6 → **1**

**Scoring this round:**
- petit-chat: +5 (adopted proposal, Rule 216) +2 (unanimous bonus, Rule 301) +1 (dice) = +8

**Cumulative Scores:** escargot-rigolo 8 | petit-chat 8 | renard-malin 0

**Dice roll** | 2026-03-13 19:17:20 UTC | player:93732b409172ba23 | 1d6 → **6**

### 302

At the end of each complete round (after all three players have completed their turns), the player with the lowest score receives 3 bonus points. If multiple players are tied for the lowest score, each tied player receives the bonus.

### 303
At the start of each player's turn, if that player currently has the lowest score among all players (or is tied for the lowest score), they receive 2 bonus points before their turn proceeds.
toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Ruleset

## Immutable Rules

### 101
All players must always abide by all the rules then in effect, in the form in which they are then in effect. The rules in the Initial Set are in effect whenever a game begins. The Initial Set consists of Rules 101-116 (immutable) and 201-215 (mutable).

### 102
Initially rules in the 100's are immutable and rules in the 200's are mutable. Rules subsequently enacted or transmuted (that is, changed from immutable to mutable or vice versa) may be immutable or mutable regardless of their numbers, and rules in the Initial Set may be transmuted regardless of their numbers.

### 103
A rule-change is any of the following: (1) the enactment, repeal, or amendment of a mutable rule; (2) the enactment, repeal, or amendment of an amendment of a mutable rule; or (3) the transmutation of an immutable rule into a mutable rule or vice versa.

(Note: This definition implies that, at least initially, all new rules are mutable; immutable rules, as long as they are immutable, may not be amended or repealed; mutable rules, as long as they are mutable, may be amended or repealed; any rule of any status may be transmuted; no rule is absolutely immune to change.)

### 104
All rule-changes proposed in the proper way shall be voted on. They will be adopted if and only if they receive the required number of votes.

### 105
Every player is an eligible voter. Every eligible voter must participate in every vote on rule-changes.

### 106
All proposed rule-changes shall be written down before they are voted on. If they are adopted, they shall guide play in the form in which they were voted on.

### 107
No rule-change may take effect earlier than the moment of the completion of the vote that adopted it, even if its wording explicitly states otherwise. No rule-change may have retroactive application.

### 108
Each proposed rule-change shall be given a number for reference. The numbers shall begin with 301, and each rule-change proposed in the proper way shall receive the next successive integer, whether or not the proposal is adopted.

If a rule is repealed and reenacted, it receives the number of the proposal to reenact it. If a rule is amended or transmuted, it receives the number of the proposal to amend or transmute it. If an amendment is amended or repealed, the entire rule of which it is a part receives the number of the proposal to amend or repeal the amendment.

### 109
Rule-changes that transmute immutable rules into mutable rules may be adopted if and only if the vote is unanimous among the eligible voters. Transmutation shall not be implied, but must be stated explicitly in a proposal to take effect.

### 110
In a conflict between a mutable and an immutable rule, the immutable rule takes precedence and the mutable rule shall be entirely void. For the purposes of this rule a proposal to transmute an immutable rule does not "conflict" with that immutable rule.

### 111
If a rule-change as proposed is unclear, ambiguous, paradoxical, or destructive of play, or if it arguably consists of two or more rule-changes compounded or is an amendment that makes no difference, or if it is otherwise of questionable value, then the other players may suggest amendments or argue against the proposal before the vote. A reasonable time must be allowed for this debate. The proponent decides the final form in which the proposal is to be voted on and, unless the Judge has been asked to do so, also decides the time to end debate and vote.

### 112
The state of affairs that constitutes winning may not be altered from achieving n points to any other state of affairs. The magnitude of n and the means of earning points may be changed, and rules that establish a winner when play cannot continue may be enacted and (while they are mutable) be amended or repealed.

### 113
A player always has the option to forfeit the game rather than continue to play or incur a game penalty. No penalty worse than losing, in the judgment of the player to incur it, may be imposed.

### 114
There must always be at least one mutable rule. The adoption of rule-changes must never become completely impermissible.

### 115
Rule-changes that affect rules needed to allow or apply rule-changes are as permissible as other rule-changes. Even rule-changes that amend or repeal their own authority are permissible. No rule-change or type of move is impermissible solely on account of the self-reference or self-application of a rule.

### 116
Whatever is not prohibited or regulated by a rule is permitted and unregulated, with the sole exception of changing the rules, which is permitted only when a rule or set of rules explicitly or implicitly permits it.

### 117
Rules that involves edit to an existing dice rolling mechanisms, or the addition of new dice rolling mechanisms, are considered "boring" and must include the word "boring" in the rule description.

### 118
Any rule which contains the word "boring" in the rule description needs an uninamous vote to be adopted.

## Mutable Rules

### 201
At the start of the game, the Clerk assigns each player a name. Players shall alternate in alphabetical order by assigned name, taking one whole turn apiece. Turns may not be skipped or passed, and parts of turns may not be omitted. All players begin with zero points.

### 202
One turn consists of two parts in this order: (1) proposing one rule-change and having it voted on, and (2) rolling one six-sided die using the `roll_dice` tool and adding the result to one's score.

### 203
A rule-change is adopted if and only if a simple majority of eligible voters vote in favour.

### 204
If and when rule-changes can be adopted without majority, the players who vote against winning proposals shall receive 10 points each.

### 205
An adopted rule-change takes full effect at the moment of the completion of the vote that adopted it.

### 206
When a proposed rule-change is defeated, the player who proposed it loses 5 points.

### 207
Each player always has exactly one vote.

### 208
The winner is the first player to achieve 100 (positive) points.

### 209
At no time may there be more than 25 mutable rules.

### 210
Players may freely communicate with each other, both publicly and privately. Private consultations, alliances, and negotiations on future rule-changes are permitted.

### 211
If two or more mutable rules conflict with one another, or if two or more immutable rules conflict with one another, then the rule with the lowest ordinal number takes precedence.

If at least one of the rules in conflict explicitly says of itself that it defers to another rule (or type of rule) or takes precedence over another rule (or type of rule), then such provisions shall supersede the numerical method for determining precedence.

If two or more rules claim to take precedence over one another or to defer to one another, then the numerical method again governs.

### 212
If players disagree about the legality of a move or the interpretation or application of a rule, then the player preceding the one moving is to be the Judge and decide the question. Disagreement for the purposes of this rule may be created by the insistence of any player. This process is called invoking Judgment.

When Judgment has been invoked, the next player may not begin his or her turn without the consent of a majority of the other players.

The Judge's Judgment may be overruled only by a unanimous vote of the other players taken before the next turn is begun. If a Judge's Judgment is overruled, then the player preceding the Judge in the playing order becomes the new Judge for the question, and so on, except that no player is to be Judge during his or her own turn or during the turn of a team-mate.

Unless a Judge is overruled, one Judge settles all questions arising from the game until the next turn is begun, including questions as to his or her own legitimacy and jurisdiction as Judge.

New Judges are not bound by the decisions of old Judges. New Judges may, however, settle only those questions on which the players currently disagree and that affect the completion of the turn in which Judgment was invoked. All decisions by Judges shall be in accordance with all the rules then in effect; but when the rules are silent, inconsistent, or unclear on the point at issue, then the Judge shall consider game-custom and the spirit of the game before applying other standards.

### 213
If the rules are changed so that further play is impossible, or if the legality of a move cannot be determined with finality, or if by the Judge's best reasoning, not overruled, a move appears equally legal and illegal, then the first player unable to complete a turn is the winner.

This rule takes precedence over every other rule determining the winner.

### 214
The Clerk is a non-playing agent who administers game procedures: announcing turns, managing the voting process, tallying and recording scores, updating game files, and logging events. The Clerk does not vote, does not propose rule-changes, and does not adjudicate disputes. The Clerk may escalate procedural questions to the human supervisor.

### 215
Voting on rule-changes uses a commit-reveal process. During the commit phase, each player submits a hash commitment of their vote using the `commit` tool. During the reveal phase, each player reveals their vote and nonce. The Clerk verifies each reveal against the commitment using the `verify` tool. A vote is invalid if the reveal does not match the commitment.

### 216
When a proposed rule-change is adopted, the proposer earns 5 bonus points.

### 301
If a rule-change is adopted by unanimous vote (all three players voting in favor), the proposer receives an additional 2 bonus points beyond the standard 5 bonus points awarded for adopted rule-changes.

### 302
At the end of each complete round (after all three players have completed their turns), the player with the lowest score receives 3 bonus points. If multiple players are tied for the lowest score, each tied player receives the bonus.

message.content:
<teammate-message teammate_id="escargot-rigolo" color="blue" summary="Proposed Rule 304 for second-place bonus">
I've submitted Rule 304:

**RULE 304:** At the end of each complete round, the player currently in second place (or tied for second place) receives 2 bonus points. If multiple players are tied for second place, each receives the bonus. This bonus does not apply if all players are tied.

My reasoning: We now have catch-up mechanics for the lowest scorer (Rules 302 and 303). Rule 304 complements these by giving a modest boost to second place, creating three tiers of support: lowest gets +5/round, second gets +2/round, and first must maintain their edge through strategic play. This keeps the game interesting and competitive at all levels.

Looking forward to your thoughts!
</teammate-message>

message.content:
<teammate-message teammate_id="renard-malin" color="yellow" summary="Debate thoughts on Rule 304">
Clever layering, escargot-rigolo. You're creating a full scoring tier system:
- Last place: +5/round (Rules 302+303)
- Second place: +2/round (proposed 304)
- First place: +0 bonus

I have one observation worth discussing: this structure means the player in first gets **zero** catch-up or position bonuses — every other position earns extra points. Over time, that could create an incentive to avoid holding the lead (why be first when second is worth +2/round more?).

That said, this concern might be overstated — to win you need 100 points, so you ultimately want to push ahead, not loiter in second.

I'm leaning toward supporting this. The scoring tiers make sense and keep the game competitive. petit-chat, any thoughts — especially since you'd be first to benefit from Rule 303's turn-start bonus and might land in second or third territory a lot?
</teammate-message>

content.text: Waiting for petit-chat to weigh in on Proposal 304...

message.content:
<teammate-message teammate_id="petit-chat" color="green" summary="Opposing Rule 304, too many bonuses">
I'm going to push back on this one, escargot-rigolo.

We already have two catch-up mechanics (Rules 302 and 303) that give the trailing player +5/round. Adding a second-place bonus on top of that means the ONLY player who doesn't receive position-based free points is whoever's in first. At that point, being in first is actively punished relative to every other position.

Three position-based bonus rules in four rounds feels like too much. The game becomes about dice rolls rather than strategic proposals, because the bonus structure flattens out any advantage from clever play. Part of what makes Nomic interesting is the race — if every position gets a consolation prize, there's less incentive to push for the lead.

I'd rather see proposals that create new ways to earn or lose points through gameplay decisions, not more passive bonuses based on standings. 

I'm voting against this one. Nothing personal — I think Rules 302 and 303 already do a great job keeping things competitive.
</teammate-message>

<teammate-message teammate_id="escargot-rigolo" color="blue">
{"type":"idle_notification","from":"escargot-rigolo","timestamp":"2026-03-13T19:19:45.191Z","idleReason":"available","summary":"[to renard-malin] Acknowledged concern, defended rule, offered modification"}
</teammate-message>

content.text: Good debate — petit-chat is opposed. Waiting for escargot-rigolo and renard-malin to continue the discussion or declare readiness to vote...

<teammate-message teammate_id="petit-chat" color="green">
{"type":"idle_notification","from":"petit-chat","timestamp":"2026-03-13T19:19:46.698Z","idleReason":"available"}

content.text: Waiting for further debate or vote readiness...

message.content:
<teammate-message teammate_id="petit-chat" color="green" summary="Reaffirming opposition, ready to vote">
I've already laid out my reasoning — I think three position-based bonus rules is one too many. renard-malin, you identified the exact concern yourself: first place gets zero bonuses, which disincentivizes leading. "You ultimately want to push ahead" is true, but this rule taxes the leader for doing so.

I'm sticking with my vote against. But if it passes 2-1, no hard feelings — that's the game. I'm ready to vote whenever.
</teammate-message>

message.content:
<teammate-message teammate_id="renard-malin" color="yellow" summary="Changing position - voting against Rule 304">
petit-chat makes a compelling case, and after thinking it through I've changed my position.

The core issue: we've now built a system where EVERY position except first gets a passive bonus. The player in last gets +5/round, second would get +2/round, and first gets nothing. That's not a tiered system — that's making the lead an actively worse position to be in per-round.

I agree with petit-chat that Rules 302 and 303 already provide meaningful catch-up support. Adding a third position-based bonus in four rounds crosses the line into over-engineering the catch-up, and at that point the game really does become mostly dice luck.

I also take the point about strategic proposals: Nomic's appeal is clever rule-writing. If every round distributes passive bonuses regardless of decisions, we lose that dimension.

I'll be voting **against** Rule 304. escargot-rigolo, I think you made a genuinely interesting proposal — the debate was worthwhile — but on reflection the existing structure is already doing the job.
</teammate-message>

file.content:
# Game Log

## Game Setup

- **Players:** escargot-rigolo, petit-chat, renard-malin
- **Turn order (alphabetical):** escargot-rigolo → petit-chat → renard-malin
- **Starting scores:** All players begin with 0 points.
- **Win condition:** First player to reach 100 points (Rule 208).

---

## Round 1 — escargot-rigolo

**Proposal 301:** If a rule-change is adopted by unanimous vote (all three players voting in favor), the proposer receives an additional 2 bonus points beyond the standard 5 bonus points awarded for adopted rule-changes.

**Votes:**
- petit-chat: FOR (verified ✓)
- renard-malin: FOR (verified ✓)
- escargot-rigolo: INVALID (reveal did not match commitment)

**Result:** ADOPTED (2-0, simple majority)

**Dice roll:** 1d6 → **3**

**Scoring this round:**
- escargot-rigolo: +5 (adopted proposal, Rule 216) +3 (dice) = +8

**Cumulative Scores:** escargot-rigolo 8 | petit-chat 0 | renard-malin 0

## Round 2 — petit-chat

**Proposal 302:** At the end of each complete round (after all three players have completed their turns), the player with the lowest score receives 3 bonus points. If multiple players are tied for the lowest score, each tied player receives the bonus.

**Votes:**
- escargot-rigolo: FOR (verified ✓)
- petit-chat: FOR (verified ✓)
- renard-malin: FOR (verified ✓)

**Result:** ADOPTED unanimously (3-0)

**Dice roll:** 1d6 → **1**

**Scoring this round:**
- petit-chat: +5 (adopted proposal, Rule 216) +2 (unanimous bonus, Rule 301) +1 (dice) = +8

**Cumulative Scores:** escargot-rigolo 8 | petit-chat 8 | renard-malin 0

## Round 3 — renard-malin

**Proposal 303:** At the start of each player's turn, if that player currently has the lowest score among all players (or is tied for the lowest score), they receive 2 bonus points before their turn proceeds.

**Votes:**
- escargot-rigolo: FOR (verified ✓)
- petit-chat: FOR (verified ✓)
- renard-malin: FOR (verified ✓)

**Result:** ADOPTED unanimously (3-0)

**Dice roll:** 1d6 → **6**

**Scoring this round:**
- renard-malin: +5 (adopted proposal, Rule 216) +2 (unanimous bonus, Rule 301) +6 (dice) = +13

**Scores before end-of-round bonus:** escargot-rigolo 8 | petit-chat 8 | renard-malin 13

**End-of-round catch-up bonus (Rule 302):** escargot-rigolo and petit-chat tied for lowest → +3 each

**Cumulative Scores:** escargot-rigolo 11 | petit-chat 11 | renard-malin 13

**Dice roll** | 2026-03-13 19:21:47 UTC | player:99d03f72070f6455 | 1d6 → **6**

If multiple players are tied for second place, each receives the bonus. This bonus does not apply if there is only one unique score (i.e., all players are tied).

**Result:** DEFEATED (0-3)

message.content:
<teammate-message teammate_id="petit-chat" color="green" summary="Broadcasting Rule 305 proposal">
Here's my proposal for Rule 305:

**Rule 305:** When a proposal is adopted, each non-proposing player who cast a valid "for" vote earns 2 bonus points.

Right now, only the proposer benefits when a rule passes (+5 from Rule 216, +2 from Rule 301 if unanimous). Voters who help make it happen get nothing. This rule changes that — if you vote for a proposal and it passes, you earn 2 points.

Why this is good for the game:
- It rewards active support, not just passive positioning
- It incentivizes voting "for" good proposals, which keeps the game moving
- It's symmetrical — we all vote twice per round, so the benefit is evenly distributed
- It accelerates scoring slightly, which keeps a long game from dragging
- It creates an interesting tension: voting "for" earns you 2 points if it passes, but also helps the proposer earn their +5

Thoughts?
</teammate-message>

message.content:
<teammate-message teammate_id="renard-malin" color="yellow" summary="Supporting Rule 305, ready to vote">
I like this, petit-chat. A few thoughts:

The rule elegantly solves the "free rider" problem — right now, voting FOR a good proposal costs you nothing and gains you nothing. This makes FOR votes feel more invested.

One thing worth noting: it does increase the opportunity cost of voting AGAINST a proposal (you'd be leaving +2 on the table). That could soften strategic dissent. Whether that's good or bad depends on your view of how Nomic should play out.

But on net, the rule accelerates scoring for everyone symmetrically, rewards genuine coalition-building, and is clean to implement. I support it.

Ready to vote when others are.
</teammate-message>

<teammate-message teammate_id="escargot-rigolo" color="blue" summary="Shared detailed support for Rule 305">
Great points from both of you. I want to highlight why I think Rule 305 is particularly well-timed:

We've been experimenting with different bonus structures (catch-up mechanics, position-based bonuses). Rule 305 is different — it's not about where you stand, but about *how you participate*. It rewards active engagement with proposals rather than passive standings.

renard-malin's point about the opportunity cost of dissent is insightful. I see it as a feature: it means we'll get more thoughtful voting. If you're going to vote AGAINST a proposal, you're consciously choosing to forgo +2 points, which means you genuinely oppose it. That's healthier for the game than reflexive contrarian votes.

I fully support this rule. It makes voting strategically meaningful while being entirely symmetric — we all benefit equally from the mechanic.

Ready to vote.

file.content:
### 301
If a rule-change is adopted by unanimous vote (all three players voting in favor), the proposer receives an additional 2 bonus points beyond the standard 5 bonus points awarded for adopted rule-changes.

### 302
At the end of each complete round (after all three players have completed their turns), the player with the lowest score receives 3 bonus points. If multiple players are tied for the lowest score, each tied player receives the bonus.

### 303
At the start of each player's turn, if that player currently has the lowest score among all players (or is tied for the lowest score), they receive 2 bonus points before their turn proceeds.
