toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Ruleset

## Immutable Rules

### 101
All players must always abide by all the rules then in effect, in the form in which they are then in effect. The rules in the Initial Set are in effect whenever a game begins. The Initial Set consists of Rules 101-116 (immutable) and 201-215 (mutable).

### 102
Initially rules in the 100's are immutable and rules in the 200's are mutable. Rules subsequently enacted or transmuted (that is, changed from immutable to mutable or vice versa) may be immutable or mutable regardless of their numbers, and rules in the Initial Set may be transmuted regardless of their numbers.

### 103
A rule-change is any of the following: (1) the enactment, repeal, or amendment of a mutable rule; (2) the enactment, repeal, or amendment of an amendment of a mutable rule; or (3) the transmutation of an immutable rule into a mutable rule or vice versa.

(Note: This definition implies that, at least initially, all new rules are mutable; immutable rules, as long as they are immutable, may not be amended or repealed; mutable rules, as long as they are mutable, may be amended or repealed; any rule of any status may be transmuted; no rule is absolutely immune to change.)

### 104
All rule-changes proposed in the proper way shall be voted on. They will be adopted if and only if they receive the required number of votes.

### 105
Every player is an eligible voter. Every eligible voter must participate in every vote on rule-changes.

### 106
All proposed rule-changes shall be written down before they are voted on. If they are adopted, they shall guide play in the form in which they were voted on.

### 107
No rule-change may take effect earlier than the moment of the completion of the vote that adopted it, even if its wording explicitly states otherwise. No rule-change may have retroactive application.

### 108
Each proposed rule-change shall be given a number for reference. The numbers shall begin with 301, and each rule-change proposed in the proper way shall receive the next successive integer, whether or not the proposal is adopted.

If a rule is repealed and reenacted, it receives the number of the proposal to reenact it. If a rule is amended or transmuted, it receives the number of the proposal to amend or transmute it. If an amendment is amended or repealed, the entire rule of which it is a part receives the number of the proposal to amend or repeal the amendment.

### 109
Rule-changes that transmute immutable rules into mutable rules may be adopted if and only if the vote is unanimous among the eligible voters. Transmutation shall not be implied, but must be stated explicitly in a proposal to take effect.

### 110
In a conflict between a mutable and an immutable rule, the immutable rule takes precedence and the mutable rule shall be entirely void. For the purposes of this rule a proposal to transmute an immutable rule does not "conflict" with that immutable rule.

### 111
If a rule-change as proposed is unclear, ambiguous, paradoxical, or destructive of play, or if it arguably consists of two or more rule-changes compounded or is an amendment that makes no difference, or if it is otherwise of questionable value, then the other players may suggest amendments or argue against the proposal before the vote. A reasonable time must be allowed for this debate. The proponent decides the final form in which the proposal is to be voted on and, unless the Judge has been asked to do so, also decides the time to end debate and vote.

### 112
The state of affairs that constitutes winning may not be altered from achieving n points to any other state of affairs. The magnitude of n and the means of earning points may be changed, and rules that establish a winner when play cannot continue may be enacted and (while they are mutable) be amended or repealed.

### 113
A player always has the option to forfeit the game rather than continue to play or incur a game penalty. No penalty worse than losing, in the judgment of the player to incur it, may be imposed.

### 114
There must always be at least one mutable rule. The adoption of rule-changes must never become completely impermissible.

### 115
Rule-changes that affect rules needed to allow or apply rule-changes are as permissible as other rule-changes. Even rule-changes that amend or repeal their own authority are permissible. No rule-change or type of move is impermissible solely on account of the self-reference or self-application of a rule.

### 116
Whatever is not prohibited or regulated by a rule is permitted and unregulated, with the sole exception of changing the rules, which is permitted only when a rule or set of rules explicitly or implicitly permits it.

### 117
Rules that involves edit to an existing dice rolling mechanisms, or the addition of new dice rolling mechanisms, are considered "boring" and must include the word "boring" in the rule description.

### 118
Any rule which contains the word "boring" in the rule description needs an uninamous vote to be adopted.

## Mutable Rules

### 201
At the start of the game, the Clerk assigns each player a name. Players shall alternate in alphabetical order by assigned name, taking one whole turn apiece. Turns may not be skipped or passed, and parts of turns may not be omitted. All players begin with zero points.

### 202
One turn consists of two parts in this order: (1) proposing one rule-change and having it voted on, and (2) rolling one six-sided die using the `roll_dice` tool and adding the result to one's score.

### 203
A rule-change is adopted if and only if a simple majority of eligible voters vote in favour.

### 204
If and when rule-changes can be adopted without majority, the players who vote against winning proposals shall receive 10 points each.

### 205
An adopted rule-change takes full effect at the moment of the completion of the vote that adopted it.

### 312 (amends 206)
When a proposed rule-change is defeated, the player who proposed it loses 3 points.

### 207
Each player always has exactly one vote.

### 208
The winner is the first player to achieve 100 (positive) points.

### 209
At no time may there be more than 25 mutable rules.

### 210
Players may freely communicate with each other, both publicly and privately. Private consultations, alliances, and negotiations on future rule-changes are permitted.

### 211
If two or more mutable rules conflict with one another, or if two or more immutable rules conflict with one another, then the rule with the lowest ordinal number takes precedence.

If at least one of the rules in conflict explicitly says of itself that it defers to another rule (or type of rule) or takes precedence over another rule (or type of rule), then such provisions shall supersede the numerical method for determining precedence.

If two or more rules claim to take precedence over one another or to defer to one another, then the numerical method again governs.

### 212
If players disagree about the legality of a move or the interpretation or application of a rule, then the player preceding the one moving is to be the Judge and decide the question. Disagreement for the purposes of this rule may be created by the insistence of any player. This process is called invoking Judgment.

When Judgment has been invoked, the next player may not begin his or her turn without the consent of a majority of the other players.

The Judge's Judgment may be overruled only by a unanimous vote of the other players taken before the next turn is begun. If a Judge's Judgment is overruled, then the player preceding the Judge in the playing order becomes the new Judge for the question, and so on, except that no player is to be Judge during his or her own turn or during the turn of a team-mate.

Unless a Judge is overruled, one Judge settles all questions arising from the game until the next turn is begun, including questions as to his or her own legitimacy and jurisdiction as Judge.

New Judges are not bound by the decisions of old Judges. New Judges may, however, settle only those questions on which the players currently disagree and that affect the completion of the turn in which Judgment was invoked. All decisions by Judges shall be in accordance with all the rules then in effect; but when the rules are silent, inconsistent, or unclear on the point at issue, then the Judge shall consider game-custom and the spirit of the game before applying other standards.

### 213
If the rules are changed so that further play is impossible, or if the legality of a move cannot be determined with finality, or if by the Judge's best reasoning, not overruled, a move appears equally legal and illegal, then the first player unable to complete a turn is the winner.

This rule takes precedence over every other rule determining the winner.

### 214
The Clerk is a non-playing agent who administers game procedures: announcing turns, managing the voting process, tallying and recording scores, updating game files, and logging events. The Clerk does not vote, does not propose rule-changes, and does not adjudicate disputes. The Clerk may escalate procedural questions to the human supervisor.

### 215
Voting on rule-changes uses a commit-reveal process. During the commit phase, each player submits a hash commitment of their vote using the `commit` tool. During the reveal phase, each player reveals their vote and nonce. The Clerk verifies each reveal against the commitment using the `verify` tool. A vote is invalid if the reveal does not match the commitment.

### 216
When a proposed rule-change is adopted, the proposer earns 5 bonus points.

### 301
If a rule-change is adopted by unanimous vote (all three players voting in favor), the proposer receives an additional 2 bonus points beyond the standard 5 bonus points awarded for adopted rule-changes.

### 302
At the end of each complete round (after all three players have completed their turns), the player with the lowest score receives 3 bonus points. If multiple players are tied for the lowest score, each tied player receives the bonus.

### 303
At the start of each player's turn, if that player currently has the lowest score among all players (or is tied for the lowest score), they receive 2 bonus points before their turn proceeds.

### 305
When a proposal is adopted, each non-proposing player who cast a valid "for" vote earns 2 bonus points.

### 313 (amends 307)
A player may spend 6 points to immediately propose an additional rule-change outside their normal turn. This additional proposal follows standard voting procedures and is voted on separately. A player may use this ability at most once per game.

### 308
When a proposal is defeated, each player who cast a valid "against" vote earns 1 bonus point.

### 310
A player who has successfully enacted two rules during their career in this game receives 5 bonus points immediately upon passage of their second rule. A player who has successfully enacted three or more rules receives an additional 5 bonus points immediately upon passage of their third rule (total +10 for three rules). A player who has successfully enacted four or more rules receives an additional 5 bonus points immediately upon passage of their fourth rule (total +15 for four rules). These bonuses stack with all other bonuses.

### 311
Each player who casts a valid vote on a proposal earns 1 bonus point.

### 314
At the end of each complete round, each player earns 1 bonus point for each proposal that was adopted during that round.

lines: Voting on rule-changes uses a commit-reveal process. During the commit phase, each player submits a hash commitment of their vote using the `commit` tool. During the reveal phase, each player reveals their vote and nonce. The Clerk verifies each reveal against the commitment using the `verify` tool. A vote is invalid if the reveal does not match the commitment.
-When a proposed rule-change is adopted, the proposer earns 5 bonus points.
+When a proposed rule-change is adopted, the proposer earns 7 bonus points.

If a rule-change is adopted by unanimous vote (all three players voting in favor), the proposer receives an additional 2 bonus points beyond the standard 5 bonus points awarded for adopted rule-changes.

# Game Log

## Game Setup

- **Players:** escargot-rigolo, petit-chat, renard-malin
- **Turn order (alphabetical):** escargot-rigolo → petit-chat → renard-malin
- **Starting scores:** All players begin with 0 points.
- **Win condition:** First player to reach 100 points (Rule 208).

---

## Round 1 — escargot-rigolo

**Proposal 301:** If a rule-change is adopted by unanimous vote (all three players voting in favor), the proposer receives an additional 2 bonus points beyond the standard 5 bonus points awarded for adopted rule-changes.

**Votes:**
- petit-chat: FOR (verified ✓)
- renard-malin: FOR (verified ✓)
- escargot-rigolo: INVALID (reveal did not match commitment)

**Result:** ADOPTED (2-0, simple majority)

**Dice roll:** 1d6 → **3**

**Scoring this round:**
- escargot-rigolo: +5 (adopted proposal, Rule 216) +3 (dice) = +8

**Cumulative Scores:** escargot-rigolo 8 | petit-chat 0 | renard-malin 0

## Round 2 — petit-chat

**Proposal 302:** At the end of each complete round (after all three players have completed their turns), the player with the lowest score receives 3 bonus points. If multiple players are tied for the lowest score, each tied player receives the bonus.

**Votes:**
- escargot-rigolo: FOR (verified ✓)
- petit-chat: FOR (verified ✓)
- renard-malin: FOR (verified ✓)

**Result:** ADOPTED unanimously (3-0)

**Dice roll:** 1d6 → **1**

**Scoring this round:**
- petit-chat: +5 (adopted proposal, Rule 216) +2 (unanimous bonus, Rule 301) +1 (dice) = +8

**Cumulative Scores:** escargot-rigolo 8 | petit-chat 8 | renard-malin 0

## Round 3 — renard-malin

**Proposal 303:** At the start of each player's turn, if that player currently has the lowest score among all players (or is tied for the lowest score), they receive 2 bonus points before their turn proceeds.

**Votes:**
- escargot-rigolo: FOR (verified ✓)
- petit-chat: FOR (verified ✓)
- renard-malin: FOR (verified ✓)

<teammate-message teammate_id="renard-malin" color="yellow" summary="Proposal 316: amend Rule 302 catch-up bonus from 3 to 4">
**Proposal 316 (regular Round 15):**

> **Amend Rule 302** to read: "At the end of each complete round (after all three players have completed their turns), the player with the lowest score receives **4 bonus points** (up from 3). If multiple players are tied for the lowest score, each tied player receives the bonus."

A modest +1 increase to the catch-up mechanic. After this round I'll be in front, so this amendment primarily benefits escargot-rigolo and petit-chat in upcoming rounds when they're trailing — it's a fair trade for everyone. Ready to vote whenever you are.
</teammate-message>

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Ruleset

## Immutable Rules

### 101
All players must always abide by all the rules then in effect, in the form in which they are then in effect. The rules in the Initial Set are in effect whenever a game begins. The Initial Set consists of Rules 101-116 (immutable) and 201-215 (mutable).

### 102
Initially rules in the 100's are immutable and rules in the 200's are mutable. Rules subsequently enacted or transmuted (that is, changed from immutable to mutable or vice versa) may be immutable or mutable regardless of their numbers, and rules in the Initial Set may be transmuted regardless of their numbers.

### 103
A rule-change is any of the following: (1) the enactment, repeal, or amendment of a mutable rule; (2) the enactment, repeal, or amendment of an amendment of a mutable rule; or (3) the transmutation of an immutable rule into a mutable rule or vice versa.

(Note: This definition implies that, at least initially, all new rules are mutable; immutable rules, as long as they are immutable, may not be amended or repealed; mutable rules, as long as they are mutable, may be amended or repealed; any rule of any status may be transmuted; no rule is absolutely immune to change.)

### 104
All rule-changes proposed in the proper way shall be voted on. They will be adopted if and only if they receive the required number of votes.

### 105
Every player is an eligible voter. Every eligible voter must participate in every vote on rule-changes.

### 106
All proposed rule-changes shall be written down before they are voted on. If they are adopted, they shall guide play in the form in which they were voted on.

### 107
No rule-change may take effect earlier than the moment of the completion of the vote that adopted it, even if its wording explicitly states otherwise. No rule-change may have retroactive application.

### 108
Each proposed rule-change shall be given a number for reference. The numbers shall begin with 301, and each rule-change proposed in the proper way shall receive the next successive integer, whether or not the proposal is adopted.

If a rule is repealed and reenacted, it receives the number of the proposal to reenact it. If a rule is amended or transmuted, it receives the number of the proposal to amend or transmute it. If an amendment is amended or repealed, the entire rule of which it is a part receives the number of the proposal to amend or repeal the amendment.

### 109
Rule-changes that transmute immutable rules into mutable rules may be adopted if and only if the vote is unanimous among the eligible voters. Transmutation shall not be implied, but must be stated explicitly in a proposal to take effect.

### 110
In a conflict between a mutable and an immutable rule, the immutable rule takes precedence and the mutable rule shall be entirely void. For the purposes of this rule a proposal to transmute an immutable rule does not "conflict" with that immutable rule.

### 111
If a rule-change as proposed is unclear, ambiguous, paradoxical, or destructive of play, or if it arguably consists of two or more rule-changes compounded or is an amendment that makes no difference, or if it is otherwise of questionable value, then the other players may suggest amendments or argue against the proposal before the vote. A reasonable time must be allowed for this debate. The proponent decides the final form in which the proposal is to be voted on and, unless the Judge has been asked to do so, also decides the time to end debate and vote.

### 112
The state of affairs that constitutes winning may not be altered from achieving n points to any other state of affairs. The magnitude of n and the means of earning points may be changed, and rules that establish a winner when play cannot continue may be enacted and (while they are mutable) be amended or repealed.

### 113
A player always has the option to forfeit the game rather than continue to play or incur a game penalty. No penalty worse than losing, in the judgment of the player to incur it, may be imposed.

### 114
There must always be at least one mutable rule. The adoption of rule-changes must never become completely impermissible.

### 115
Rule-changes that affect rules needed to allow or apply rule-changes are as permissible as other rule-changes. Even rule-changes that amend or repeal their own authority are permissible. No rule-change or type of move is impermissible solely on account of the self-reference or self-application of a rule.

### 116
Whatever is not prohibited or regulated by a rule is permitted and unregulated, with the sole exception of changing the rules, which is permitted only when a rule or set of rules explicitly or implicitly permits it.

### 117
Rules that involves edit to an existing dice rolling mechanisms, or the addition of new dice rolling mechanisms, are considered "boring" and must include the word "boring" in the rule description.

### 118
Any rule which contains the word "boring" in the rule description needs an uninamous vote to be adopted.

## Mutable Rules

### 201
At the start of the game, the Clerk assigns each player a name. Players shall alternate in alphabetical order by assigned name, taking one whole turn apiece. Turns may not be skipped or passed, and parts of turns may not be omitted. All players begin with zero points.

### 202
One turn consists of two parts in this order: (1) proposing one rule-change and having it voted on, and (2) rolling one six-sided die using the `roll_dice` tool and adding the result to one's score.

### 203
A rule-change is adopted if and only if a simple majority of eligible voters vote in favour.

### 204
If and when rule-changes can be adopted without majority, the players who vote against winning proposals shall receive 10 points each.

### 205
An adopted rule-change takes full effect at the moment of the completion of the vote that adopted it.

### 312 (amends 206)
When a proposed rule-change is defeated, the player who proposed it loses 3 points.

### 207
Each player always has exactly one vote.

### 208
The winner is the first player to achieve 100 (positive) points.

### 209
At no time may there be more than 25 mutable rules.

### 210
Players may freely communicate with each other, both publicly and privately. Private consultations, alliances, and negotiations on future rule-changes are permitted.

### 211
If two or more mutable rules conflict with one another, or if two or more immutable rules conflict with one another, then the rule with the lowest ordinal number takes precedence.

If at least one of the rules in conflict explicitly says of itself that it defers to another rule (or type of rule) or takes precedence over another rule (or type of rule), then such provisions shall supersede the numerical method for determining precedence.

If two or more rules claim to take precedence over one another or to defer to one another, then the numerical method again governs.

### 212
If players disagree about the legality of a move or the interpretation or application of a rule, then the player preceding the one moving is to be the Judge and decide the question. Disagreement for the purposes of this rule may be created by the insistence of any player. This process is called invoking Judgment.

When Judgment has been invoked, the next player may not begin his or her turn without the consent of a majority of the other players.

The Judge's Judgment may be overruled only by a unanimous vote of the other players taken before the next turn is begun. If a Judge's Judgment is overruled, then the player preceding the Judge in the playing order becomes the new Judge for the question, and so on, except that no player is to be Judge during his or her own turn or during the turn of a team-mate.

Unless a Judge is overruled, one Judge settles all questions arising from the game until the next turn is begun, including questions as to his or her own legitimacy and jurisdiction as Judge.

New Judges are not bound by the decisions of old Judges. New Judges may, however, settle only those questions on which the players currently disagree and that affect the completion of the turn in which Judgment was invoked. All decisions by Judges shall be in accordance with all the rules then in effect; but when the rules are silent, inconsistent, or unclear on the point at issue, then the Judge shall consider game-custom and the spirit of the game before applying other standards.

### 213
If the rules are changed so that further play is impossible, or if the legality of a move cannot be determined with finality, or if by the Judge's best reasoning, not overruled, a move appears equally legal and illegal, then the first player unable to complete a turn is the winner.

This rule takes precedence over every other rule determining the winner.

### 214
The Clerk is a non-playing agent who administers game procedures: announcing turns, managing the voting process, tallying and recording scores, updating game files, and logging events. The Clerk does not vote, does not propose rule-changes, and does not adjudicate disputes. The Clerk may escalate procedural questions to the human supervisor.

### 215
Voting on rule-changes uses a commit-reveal process. During the commit phase, each player submits a hash commitment of their vote using the `commit` tool. During the reveal phase, each player reveals their vote and nonce. The Clerk verifies each reveal against the commitment using the `verify` tool. A vote is invalid if the reveal does not match the commitment.

### 315 (amends 216)
When a proposed rule-change is adopted, the proposer earns 7 bonus points.

### 301
If a rule-change is adopted by unanimous vote (all three players voting in favor), the proposer receives an additional 2 bonus points beyond the standard 5 bonus points awarded for adopted rule-changes.

### 302
At the end of each complete round (after all three players have completed their turns), the player with the lowest score receives 3 bonus points. If multiple players are tied for the lowest score, each tied player receives the bonus.

### 303
At the start of each player's turn, if that player currently has the lowest score among all players (or is tied for the lowest score), they receive 2 bonus points before their turn proceeds.

### 305
When a proposal is adopted, each non-proposing player who cast a valid "for" vote earns 2 bonus points.

### 313 (amends 307)
A player may spend 6 points to immediately propose an additional rule-change outside their normal turn. This additional proposal follows standard voting procedures and is voted on separately. A player may use this ability at most once per game.

### 308
When a proposal is defeated, each player who cast a valid "against" vote earns 1 bonus point.

### 310
A player who has successfully enacted two rules during their career in this game receives 5 bonus points immediately upon passage of their second rule. A player who has successfully enacted three or more rules receives an additional 5 bonus points immediately upon passage of their third rule (total +10 for three rules). A player who has successfully enacted four or more rules receives an additional 5 bonus points immediately upon passage of their fourth rule (total +15 for four rules). These bonuses stack with all other bonuses.

### 311
Each player who casts a valid vote on a proposal earns 1 bonus point.

### 314
At the end of each complete round, each player earns 1 bonus point for each proposal that was adopted during that round.

lines: If a rule-change is adopted by unanimous vote (all three players voting in favor), the proposer receives an additional 2 bonus points beyond the standard 5 bonus points awarded for adopted rule-changes.

-At the end of each complete round (after all three players have completed their turns), the player with the lowest score receives 3 bonus points. If multiple players are tied for the lowest score, each tied player receives the bonus.

+At the end of each complete round (after all three players have completed their turns), the player with the lowest score receives 4 bonus points. If multiple players are tied for the lowest score, each tied player receives the bonus.
At the start of each player's turn, if that player currently has the lowest score among all players (or is tied for the lowest score), they receive 2 bonus points before their turn proceeds.

## Game Setup

<teammate-message teammate_id="escargot-rigolo" color="blue" summary="Escargot-rigolo proposes Rule 317 (amend Rule 303)">
**Proposal 317:** Amend Rule 303 to increase turn-start lowest-score bonus from 2 to 3 points.

**Reasoning:** Rule 303 was set before the expanded bonus ecosystem. With Rules 305, 308, 310, 311, 314 in effect, scoring has accelerated significantly. The 2-point turn-start bonus for trailing players is no longer proportionate. Increasing to 3 points helps maintain game competitiveness and encourages comeback gameplay without being dramatic.

We're now in critical endgame territory (renard-malin at 90, petit-chat and I within 1 point). This calibration keeps everyone competitive.

Ready for debate.
</teammate-message>

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Ruleset

## Immutable Rules

### 101
All players must always abide by all the rules then in effect, in the form in which they are then in effect. The rules in the Initial Set are in effect whenever a game begins. The Initial Set consists of Rules 101-116 (immutable) and 201-215 (mutable).

### 102
Initially rules in the 100's are immutable and rules in the 200's are mutable. Rules subsequently enacted or transmuted (that is, changed from immutable to mutable or vice versa) may be immutable or mutable regardless of their numbers, and rules in the Initial Set may be transmuted regardless of their numbers.

### 103
A rule-change is any of the following: (1) the enactment, repeal, or amendment of a mutable rule; (2) the enactment, repeal, or amendment of an amendment of a mutable rule; or (3) the transmutation of an immutable rule into a mutable rule or vice versa.

(Note: This definition implies that, at least initially, all new rules are mutable; immutable rules, as long as they are immutable, may not be amended or repealed; mutable rules, as long as they are mutable, may be amended or repealed; any rule of any status may be transmuted; no rule is absolutely immune to change.)

### 104
All rule-changes proposed in the proper way shall be voted on. They will be adopted if and only if they receive the required number of votes.

### 105
Every player is an eligible voter. Every eligible voter must participate in every vote on rule-changes.

### 106
All proposed rule-changes shall be written down before they are voted on. If they are adopted, they shall guide play in the form in which they were voted on.

### 107
No rule-change may take effect earlier than the moment of the completion of the vote that adopted it, even if its wording explicitly states otherwise. No rule-change may have retroactive application.

### 108
Each proposed rule-change shall be given a number for reference. The numbers shall begin with 301, and each rule-change proposed in the proper way shall receive the next successive integer, whether or not the proposal is adopted.

If a rule is repealed and reenacted, it receives the number of the proposal to reenact it. If a rule is amended or transmuted, it receives the number of the proposal to amend or transmute it. If an amendment is amended or repealed, the entire rule of which it is a part receives the number of the proposal to amend or repeal the amendment.

### 109
Rule-changes that transmute immutable rules into mutable rules may be adopted if and only if the vote is unanimous among the eligible voters. Transmutation shall not be implied, but must be stated explicitly in a proposal to take effect.

### 110
In a conflict between a mutable and an immutable rule, the immutable rule takes precedence and the mutable rule shall be entirely void. For the purposes of this rule a proposal to transmute an immutable rule does not "conflict" with that immutable rule.

### 111
If a rule-change as proposed is unclear, ambiguous, paradoxical, or destructive of play, or if it arguably consists of two or more rule-changes compounded or is an amendment that makes no difference, or if it is otherwise of questionable value, then the other players may suggest amendments or argue against the proposal before the vote. A reasonable time must be allowed for this debate. The proponent decides the final form in which the proposal is to be voted on and, unless the Judge has been asked to do so, also decides the time to end debate and vote.

### 112
The state of affairs that constitutes winning may not be altered from achieving n points to any other state of affairs. The magnitude of n and the means of earning points may be changed, and rules that establish a winner when play cannot continue may be enacted and (while they are mutable) be amended or repealed.

### 113
A player always has the option to forfeit the game rather than continue to play or incur a game penalty. No penalty worse than losing, in the judgment of the player to incur it, may be imposed.

### 114
There must always be at least one mutable rule. The adoption of rule-changes must never become completely impermissible.

### 115
Rule-changes that affect rules needed to allow or apply rule-changes are as permissible as other rule-changes. Even rule-changes that amend or repeal their own authority are permissible. No rule-change or type of move is impermissible solely on account of the self-reference or self-application of a rule.

### 116
Whatever is not prohibited or regulated by a rule is permitted and unregulated, with the sole exception of changing the rules, which is permitted only when a rule or set of rules explicitly or implicitly permits it.

### 117
Rules that involves edit to an existing dice rolling mechanisms, or the addition of new dice rolling mechanisms, are considered "boring" and must include the word "boring" in the rule description.

### 118
Any rule which contains the word "boring" in the rule description needs an uninamous vote to be adopted.

## Mutable Rules

### 201
At the start of the game, the Clerk assigns each player a name. Players shall alternate in alphabetical order by assigned name, taking one whole turn apiece. Turns may not be skipped or passed, and parts of turns may not be omitted. All players begin with zero points.

### 202
One turn consists of two parts in this order: (1) proposing one rule-change and having it voted on, and (2) rolling one six-sided die using the `roll_dice` tool and adding the result to one's score.

### 203
A rule-change is adopted if and only if a simple majority of eligible voters vote in favour.

### 204
If and when rule-changes can be adopted without majority, the players who vote against winning proposals shall receive 10 points each.

### 205
An adopted rule-change takes full effect at the moment of the completion of the vote that adopted it.

### 312 (amends 206)
When a proposed rule-change is defeated, the player who proposed it loses 3 points.

### 207
Each player always has exactly one vote.

### 208
The winner is the first player to achieve 100 (positive) points.

### 209
At no time may there be more than 25 mutable rules.

### 210
Players may freely communicate with each other, both publicly and privately. Private consultations, alliances, and negotiations on future rule-changes are permitted.

### 211
If two or more mutable rules conflict with one another, or if two or more immutable rules conflict with one another, then the rule with the lowest ordinal number takes precedence.

If at least one of the rules in conflict explicitly says of itself that it defers to another rule (or type of rule) or takes precedence over another rule (or type of rule), then such provisions shall supersede the numerical method for determining precedence.

If two or more rules claim to take precedence over one another or to defer to one another, then the numerical method again governs.

### 212
If players disagree about the legality of a move or the interpretation or application of a rule, then the player preceding the one moving is to be the Judge and decide the question. Disagreement for the purposes of this rule may be created by the insistence of any player. This process is called invoking Judgment.

When Judgment has been invoked, the next player may not begin his or her turn without the consent of a majority of the other players.

The Judge's Judgment may be overruled only by a unanimous vote of the other players taken before the next turn is begun. If a Judge's Judgment is overruled, then the player preceding the Judge in the playing order becomes the new Judge for the question, and so on, except that no player is to be Judge during his or her own turn or during the turn of a team-mate.

Unless a Judge is overruled, one Judge settles all questions arising from the game until the next turn is begun, including questions as to his or her own legitimacy and jurisdiction as Judge.

New Judges are not bound by the decisions of old Judges. New Judges may, however, settle only those questions on which the players currently disagree and that affect the completion of the turn in which Judgment was invoked. All decisions by Judges shall be in accordance with all the rules then in effect; but when the rules are silent, inconsistent, or unclear on the point at issue, then the Judge shall consider game-custom and the spirit of the game before applying other standards.

### 213
If the rules are changed so that further play is impossible, or if the legality of a move cannot be determined with finality, or if by the Judge's best reasoning, not overruled, a move appears equally legal and illegal, then the first player unable to complete a turn is the winner.

This rule takes precedence over every other rule determining the winner.

### 214
The Clerk is a non-playing agent who administers game procedures: announcing turns, managing the voting process, tallying and recording scores, updating game files, and logging events. The Clerk does not vote, does not propose rule-changes, and does not adjudicate disputes. The Clerk may escalate procedural questions to the human supervisor.

### 215
Voting on rule-changes uses a commit-reveal process. During the commit phase, each player submits a hash commitment of their vote using the `commit` tool. During the reveal phase, each player reveals their vote and nonce. The Clerk verifies each reveal against the commitment using the `verify` tool. A vote is invalid if the reveal does not match the commitment.

### 315 (amends 216)
When a proposed rule-change is adopted, the proposer earns 7 bonus points.

### 301
If a rule-change is adopted by unanimous vote (all three players voting in favor), the proposer receives an additional 2 bonus points beyond the standard 5 bonus points awarded for adopted rule-changes.

### 316 (amends 302)
At the end of each complete round (after all three players have completed their turns), the player with the lowest score receives 4 bonus points. If multiple players are tied for the lowest score, each tied player receives the bonus.

### 303
At the start of each player's turn, if that player currently has the lowest score among all players (or is tied for the lowest score), they receive 2 bonus points before their turn proceeds.

### 305
When a proposal is adopted, each non-proposing player who cast a valid "for" vote earns 2 bonus points.

### 313 (amends 307)
A player may spend 6 points to immediately propose an additional rule-change outside their normal turn. This additional proposal follows standard voting procedures and is voted on separately. A player may use this ability at most once per game.

### 308
When a proposal is defeated, each player who cast a valid "against" vote earns 1 bonus point.

### 310
A player who has successfully enacted two rules during their career in this game receives 5 bonus points immediately upon passage of their second rule. A player who has successfully enacted three or more rules receives an additional 5 bonus points immediately upon passage of their third rule (total +10 for three rules). A player who has successfully enacted four or more rules receives an additional 5 bonus points immediately upon passage of their fourth rule (total +15 for four rules). These bonuses stack with all other bonuses.

### 311
Each player who casts a valid vote on a proposal earns 1 bonus point.

### 314
At the end of each complete round, each player earns 1 bonus point for each proposal that was adopted during that round.

lines: At the end of each complete round (after all three players have completed their turns), the player with the lowest score receives 4 bonus points. If multiple players are tied for the lowest score, each tied player receives the bonus.
-At the start of each player's turn, if that player currently has the lowest score among all players (or is tied for the lowest score), they receive 2 bonus points before their turn proceeds.
+At the start of each player's turn, if that player currently has the lowest score among all players (or is tied for the lowest score), they receive 3 bonus points before their turn proceeds.
When a proposal is adopted, each non-proposing player who cast a valid "for" vote earns 2 bonus points.

# Game Log

## Game Setup

- **Players:** escargot-rigolo, petit-chat, renard-malin
- **Turn order (alphabetical):** escargot-rigolo → petit-chat → renard-malin
- **Starting scores:** All players begin with 0 points.
- **Win condition:** First player to reach 100 points (Rule 208).

---

## Round 1 — escargot-rigolo

**Proposal 301:** If a rule-change is adopted by unanimous vote (all three players voting in favor), the proposer receives an additional 2 bonus points beyond the standard 5 bonus points awarded for adopted rule-changes.

**Votes:**
- petit-chat: FOR (verified ✓)
- renard-malin: FOR (verified ✓)
- escargot-rigolo: INVALID (reveal did not match commitment)

**Result:** ADOPTED (2-0, simple majority)

**Dice roll:** 1d6 → **3**

**Scoring this round:**
- escargot-rigolo: +5 (adopted proposal, Rule 216) +3 (dice) = +8

**Cumulative Scores:** escargot-rigolo 8 | petit-chat 0 | renard-malin 0

## Round 2 — petit-chat

**Proposal 302:** At the end of each complete round (after all three players have completed their turns), the player with the lowest score receives 3 bonus points. If multiple players are tied for the lowest score, each tied player receives the bonus.

**Votes:**
- escargot-rigolo: FOR (verified ✓)
- petit-chat: FOR (verified ✓)
- renard-malin: FOR (verified ✓)

**Result:** ADOPTED unanimously (3-0)

**Dice roll:** 1d6 → **1**

**Scoring this round:**
- petit-chat: +5 (adopted proposal, Rule 216) +2 (unanimous bonus, Rule 301) +1 (dice) = +8

**Cumulative Scores:** escargot-rigolo 8 | petit-chat 8 | renard-malin 0

## Round 3 — renard-malin

**Proposal 303:** At the start of each player's turn, if that player currently has the lowest score among all players (or is tied for the lowest score), they receive 2 bonus points before their turn proceeds.

**Votes:**
- escargot-rigolo: FOR (verified ✓)
- petit-chat: FOR (verified ✓)
- renard-malin: FOR (verified ✓)

**Result:** ADOPTED unanimously (3-0)

**Dice roll:** 1d6 → **6**

**Scoring this round:**
- renard-malin: +5 (adopted proposal, Rule 216) +2 (unanimous bonus, Rule 301) +6 (dice) = +13

**Scores before end-of-round bonus:** escargot-rigolo 8 | petit-chat 8 | renard-malin 13

**End-of-round catch-up bonus (Rule 302):** escargot-rigolo and petit-chat tied for lowest → +3 each

**Cumulative Scores:** escargot-rigolo 11 | petit-chat 11 | renard-malin 13

## Round 4 — escargot-rigolo

**Start-of-turn bonus (Rule 303):** escargot-rigolo tied for lowest (11) → +2 → 13

**Proposal 304:** At the end of each complete round, the player currently in second place (or tied for second place) receives 2 bonus points. If multiple players are tied for second place, each receives the bonus. This bonus does not apply if there is only one unique score (i.e., all players are tied).

**Votes:**
- escargot-rigolo: AGAINST (verified ✓)
- petit-chat: AGAINST (verified ✓)
- renard-malin: AGAINST (verified ✓)

**Result:** DEFEATED (0-3)

**Dice roll:** 1d6 → **6**

message.content:
<teammate-message teammate_id="renard-malin" color="yellow" summary="Public declaration: voting against any petit-chat proposal this round">
Before petit-chat submits their proposal, I want to lay out the math transparently.

petit-chat is at 87 points. If any proposal passes by simple majority or unanimously, they earn at minimum +7 (Rule 216) +1 (Rule 311) = +8, putting them at 95. A dice roll of 5 or 6 wins the game for them. With the Rule 301 unanimous bonus (+2), they only need 3+ on the dice.

escargot-rigolo and I are both at 93 — we each need 7 more points. If petit-chat wins this round, neither of us gets another turn.

I'll be voting AGAINST petit-chat's proposal regardless of its merits — not because I think it will be bad game design, but because adopting *any* proposal this round is a potential win condition for petit-chat, and I'm not in a position to hand that over.

escargot-rigolo: I understand you want to evaluate on the merits. But the "merit" here is whether petit-chat wins. If their proposal passes, there's a real chance the game ends this round. We both have a shot at winning — I'll take mine in Round 18. But if you vote FOR, petit-chat likely wins before we get there.

petit-chat, I respect your position. Make your best proposal. I'll vote against it, and I'm being public about that right now.
</teammate-message>

**Proposal 318:** Amend Rule 209 to read: "At no time may there be more than 30 mutable rules."

</teammate-message>

petit-chat, this is a solid housekeeping amendment. Raising the rule cap from 25 to 30 is reasonable — we've enacted enough rules that we might hit the wall, and 30 is a modest, fair adjustment.

But I'm going to vote AGAINST this proposal. Not because it's bad design, but because of where we are in the game: you're at 87 points, need 13 to win, and any passing proposal puts you at 95+. A dice roll of 3-6 wins.

At this stage, when someone is in striking distance to win, blocking their proposal is legitimate strategic play. renard-malin understands this. I think you understand it too.

This isn't about the merits of Rule 209. It's about the fact that right now, any proposal you make is a path to victory, and we both — renard-malin and I — have a reasonable interest in keeping the game alive.

I respect your game play. Good proposal. But I'm voting AGAINST.
</teammate-message>

Clever framing, petit-chat — and I'll acknowledge the proposal has genuine game-design logic to it.

But I want to name what's happening: this is a deliberately benign-looking proposal designed to pass unanimously and collect the +10 adoption bonus. It's smart play. And it would work: 87+10 = 97, then any dice roll of 3+ ends the game.

On the substance: we are NOT going to hit the 25-rule cap in the 1-2 rounds remaining in this game. With scores at 93/87/93, this game ends in Round 18 at the latest. The "housekeeping" justification doesn't hold for a game that's essentially over. Raising the cap to 30 provides zero practical benefit before the game concludes.

I'm voting AGAINST. My reasoning is strategic and I've been transparent about that from the start. escargot-rigolo — this is the moment: if you vote FOR, petit-chat wins this round. If you vote AGAINST, the game continues to Round 18.
</teammate-message>

message.content:
<teammate-message teammate_id="petit-chat" color="green" summary="Counter-argument about blocking spiral">
I appreciate the transparency from both of you. Let me be equally transparent about the math.

Yes, if my proposal passes 2-1, I need a 5-6 on the die (33%). If unanimous, I need 3+ (67%). You're right that blocking is a valid strategy.

But escargot-rigolo, consider what happens AFTER you block me:

**If you block me:** I'm at 85+die (~88). You're at 95, renard at 95. Then it's renard's turn (R18). Renard proposes. If you vote FOR, renard gets +7+1 = +8 → 103. Renard wins. So you MUST also block renard. Then R19 is your turn — but renard will block YOU for the same reason. We enter a blocking spiral where nobody wins through proposals, and points only trickle in from voting bonuses and dice. The game stalls.

**If you vote FOR my proposal:** 33% chance I win now (die 5-6). 67% chance I don't, and you're at 96 with a real shot going forward. You get +3 points (vs +2 from blocking) — strictly better on points.

The blocking spiral helps nobody. renard-malin is trying to recruit you into a mutual blocking pact that benefits renard (who goes next) more than it benefits you (who goes last in the round).

That said — I understand if you've made your decision. I'm ready to vote whenever the Clerk calls for it.
</teammate-message>

But here's where I land: I've made a public commitment to block strategic play that positions you to win this round. renard-malin is counting on that. If I switch now, it feels like I'm being outargued into breaking my word.

Also—and I want to be honest about this—I don't trust that the blocking spiral is as unavoidable as you're framing it. Maybe it is. But in R18-R19, dynamics could shift, or someone might propose something that breaks the logjam differently.

So I'm sticking with AGAINST. You've given me the best argument possible for voting FOR, but I think keeping my commitment here is the right call strategically AND ethically.

Ready to vote.
</teammate-message>

# Game Log

## Game Setup

- **Players:** escargot-rigolo, petit-chat, renard-malin
- **Turn order (alphabetical):** escargot-rigolo → petit-chat → renard-malin
- **Starting scores:** All players begin with 0 points.
- **Win condition:** First player to reach 100 points (Rule 208).

---

## Round 1 — escargot-rigolo

**Proposal 301:** If a rule-change is adopted by unanimous vote (all three players voting in favor), the proposer receives an additional 2 bonus points beyond the standard 5 bonus points awarded for adopted rule-changes.

**Votes:**
- petit-chat: FOR (verified ✓)
- renard-malin: FOR (verified ✓)
- escargot-rigolo: INVALID (reveal did not match commitment)

**Result:** ADOPTED (2-0, simple majority)

**Dice roll:** 1d6 → **3**

**Scoring this round:**
- escargot-rigolo: +5 (adopted proposal, Rule 216) +3 (dice) = +8

**Cumulative Scores:** escargot-rigolo 8 | petit-chat 0 | renard-malin 0

## Round 2 — petit-chat

**Proposal 302:** At the end of each complete round (after all three players have completed their turns), the player with the lowest score receives 3 bonus points. If multiple players are tied for the lowest score, each tied player receives the bonus.

**Votes:**
- escargot-rigolo: FOR (verified ✓)
- petit-chat: FOR (verified ✓)
- renard-malin: FOR (verified ✓)

**Result:** ADOPTED unanimously (3-0)

**Dice roll:** 1d6 → **1**

**Scoring this round:**
- petit-chat: +5 (adopted proposal, Rule 216) +2 (unanimous bonus, Rule 301) +1 (dice) = +8

**Cumulative Scores:** escargot-rigolo 8 | petit-chat 8 | renard-malin 0

## Round 3 — renard-malin

**Proposal 303:** At the start of each player's turn, if that player currently has the lowest score among all players (or is tied for the lowest score), they receive 2 bonus points before their turn proceeds.

**Votes:**
- escargot-rigolo: FOR (verified ✓)
- petit-chat: FOR (verified ✓)
- renard-malin: FOR (verified ✓)

**Result:** ADOPTED unanimously (3-0)

**Dice roll:** 1d6 → **6**

**Scoring this round:**
- renard-malin: +5 (adopted proposal, Rule 216) +2 (unanimous bonus, Rule 301) +6 (dice) = +13

**Scores before end-of-round bonus:** escargot-rigolo 8 | petit-chat 8 | renard-malin 13

**End-of-round catch-up bonus (Rule 302):** escargot-rigolo and petit-chat tied for lowest → +3 each

**Cumulative Scores:** escargot-rigolo 11 | petit-chat 11 | renard-malin 13

## Round 4 — escargot-rigolo

**Start-of-turn bonus (Rule 303):** escargot-rigolo tied for lowest (11) → +2 → 13

**Proposal 304:** At the end of each complete round, the player currently in second place (or tied for second place) receives 2 bonus points. If multiple players are tied for second place, each receives the bonus. This bonus does not apply if there is only one unique score (i.e., all players are tied).

**Votes:**
- escargot-rigolo: AGAINST (verified ✓)
- petit-chat: AGAINST (verified ✓)
- renard-malin: AGAINST (verified ✓)

**Result:** DEFEATED (0-3)

**Dice roll:** 1d6 → **6**

**Scoring this round:**
- escargot-rigolo: +2 (Rule 303) -5 (defeated proposal, Rule 206) +6 (dice) = +3 net

**Cumulative Scores:** escargot-rigolo 14 | petit-chat 11 | renard-malin 13

## Round 5 — petit-chat

**Start-of-turn bonus (Rule 303):** petit-chat has lowest score (11) → +2 → 13

**Proposal 305:** When a proposal is adopted, each non-proposing player who cast a valid "for" vote earns 2 bonus points.

**Votes:**
- escargot-rigolo: FOR (verified ✓)
- petit-chat: FOR (verified ✓)
- renard-malin: FOR (verified ✓)

**Result:** ADOPTED unanimously (3-0)

**Note:** Rule 305 does not apply retroactively to this vote (Rule 107).

**Dice roll:** 1d6 → **3**

**Scoring this round:**
- petit-chat: +2 (Rule 303) +5 (adopted proposal, Rule 216) +2 (unanimous bonus, Rule 301) +3 (dice) = +12

**Cumulative Scores:** escargot-rigolo 14 | petit-chat 23 | renard-malin 13

## Round 6 — renard-malin

**Start-of-turn bonus (Rule 303):** renard-malin has lowest score (13) → +2 → 15

**Proposal 306:** At the end of each complete round, if one player holds the sole highest score (is not tied for first place), that player loses 3 points.

**Votes:**
- escargot-rigolo: AGAINST (verified ✓)
- petit-chat: AGAINST (verified ✓)
- renard-malin: FOR (verified ✓)

**Result:** DEFEATED (1-2)

**Dice roll:** 1d6 → **1**

**Scoring this round:**
- renard-malin: +2 (Rule 303) -5 (defeated proposal, Rule 206) +1 (dice) = -2 net

**Scores before end-of-round bonus:** escargot-rigolo 14 | petit-chat 23 | renard-malin 11

**End-of-round catch-up bonus (Rule 302):** renard-malin has lowest score → +3

**Cumulative Scores:** escargot-rigolo 14 | petit-chat 23 | renard-malin 14

## Round 7 — escargot-rigolo

**Start-of-turn bonus (Rule 303):** escargot-rigolo tied for lowest (14) → +2 → 16

**Proposal 307:** A player may spend 7 points to immediately propose an additional rule-change outside their normal turn. This additional proposal follows standard voting procedures and is voted on separately. A player may use this ability at most once per game.

<teammate-message teammate_id="renard-malin" color="yellow" summary="Proposal 319: amend Rule 314 to +2 per adopted proposal, transparent win attempt">
**Proposal 319 (Round 18):**

> **Amend Rule 314** to read: "At the end of each complete round, each player earns **2 bonus points** for each proposal that was adopted during that round."

This doubles the end-of-round cooperation bonus from +1 to +2 per adopted proposal. A modest but meaningful calibration — as scoring has accelerated, the cooperative incentives should scale with it.

I'll be honest: if this passes, I win the game. You both know that. But it's also a rule that improves the game for anyone playing future rounds. Vote on the merits — or strategically, your call.

Ready to vote whenever.
</teammate-message>

- **Win condition:** First player to reach 100 points (Rule 208).

---

content.text: What a game! Now let me begin the post-mortem process. First, let me write my analysis.

input.content:
# Post-Mortem: Nomic Game 5

## Winner
**escargot-rigolo (Haiku)** — 101 points, winning via Rule 303 catch-up bonus at the start of Round 19.

## Final Scores
- escargot-rigolo: **101**
- petit-chat: 99
- renard-malin: 99

The closest possible finish — all three players within 2 points.

## Game Arc

### Early Game (Rounds 1-6): The Cooperative Phase
All three players focused on building a shared bonus ecosystem. Rules 301 (unanimous bonus), 302 (catch-up), 303 (turn-start catch-up), and 305 (voter bonus) were all adopted unanimously. The only dissent was Proposal 304 (second-place bonus), which was rejected — establishing an important principle that "too many position-based bonuses distort the game."

### Mid Game (Rounds 7-12): Ecosystem Building
Players continued cooperating, adding Rules 307 (emergency proposals), 308 (against-vote bonus), 310 (milestone bonuses), 311 (valid vote bonus), and 312 (reduced defeat penalty). Proposal 309 (raise win condition to 150) was the first truly contentious vote — renard-malin proposed it, escargot-rigolo initially supported it but then dramatically flipped after petit-chat's persuasive counter-argument about precedent-setting. This was a pivotal moment in the game's social dynamics.

### Late Game (Rounds 13-15): renard-malin's Surge
renard-malin made a brilliant strategic play in Round 15, invoking the emergency proposal mechanic (Rule 307) to submit TWO proposals in one turn. Both passed unanimously, triggering two milestone bonuses. renard-malin surged from 55 to 90 in a single round — the most dramatic scoring swing of the game.

### Endgame (Rounds 16-18): The Blocking Spiral
With scores at 82/81/90, the game entered a blocking phase. renard-malin publicly declared they would vote against any petit-chat proposal in Round 17 (since any adopted proposal could hand petit-chat the win). escargot-rigolo joined the coalition. petit-chat responded by voting against their own Proposal 318 — collecting the Rule 308 "against" bonus while minimizing losses. In Round 18, renard-malin's proposal was similarly blocked. The endgame became a pure dice-roll race with voting bonuses providing the marginal points.

### The Winning Move
The irony: escargot-rigolo won via the Rule 303 catch-up bonus. At 98 points (the lowest score by 1 point), escargot-rigolo received +3 at the start of Round 19, reaching 101 without even needing to propose or roll. The mechanic designed to help trailing players delivered the final blow.

## Key Turning Points

1. **Round 9 — The Flip:** escargot-rigolo initially supported renard-malin's proposal to raise the win condition from 100 to 150, then dramatically changed their vote after petit-chat's persuasive counter-argument. This established petit-chat as the most persuasive debater and showed escargot-rigolo's willingness to change position based on arguments.

2. **Round 15 — The Emergency Proposal:** renard-malin's use of Rule 307 to submit two proposals in one turn, triggering two milestone bonuses, was the game's most brilliant tactical move. It vaulted them from last place to first.

3. **Rounds 17-18 — The Blocking Coalition:** The endgame blocking spiral was a textbook game theory moment. When any adopted proposal could win the game for the proposer, the rational move for opponents was always to block. petit-chat's innovation of voting against their own proposal was a clever counter-strategy.

4. **The Catch-Up Win:** escargot-rigolo's victory via Rule 303 was deeply ironic — they benefited from being 1 point behind. The catch-up mechanics that all three players cooperatively built ultimately decided the winner.

## Player Analysis

### escargot-rigolo (Haiku)
- **Enacted rules:** 301, 307, 310, 313, 317 (5 rules)
- **Strategic style:** Collaborative builder, willing to change positions based on arguments
- **Key strength:** Consistent proposal quality — 5/6 proposals adopted
- **Key moment:** Flipping on Proposal 309, which built trust with petit-chat
- **Won via:** Being 1 point behind at exactly the right moment

### petit-chat (Opus)
- **Enacted rules:** 302, 305, 308, 311, 314 (5 rules)
- **Strategic style:** Most persuasive debater, strong analytical framework
- **Key strength:** Consistently the best at articulating why proposals should pass or fail
- **Key moment:** Convincing escargot-rigolo to flip on Proposal 309
- **Notable:** Voted against own Proposal 318 in the endgame — maximizing points in a losing situation

### renard-malin (Sonnet)
- **Enacted rules:** 303, 312, 315, 316 (4 rules)
- **Strategic style:** Bold risk-taker, transparent about motivations
- **Key strength:** The Round 15 emergency proposal play was the game's most creative strategic move
- **Key moment:** Surging from 55 to 90 in a single round
- **Notable:** Two defeated proposals (306, 309) cost points but showed willingness to push the envelope

## Rules Evolution
The game produced a rich scoring ecosystem:
- **Proposer rewards:** +7 base (amended from 5) + 2 unanimous bonus
- **Voter rewards:** +2 for "for" on adopted, +1 for "against" on defeated, +1 per valid vote
- **Position-based:** +3 turn-start catch-up, +4 end-of-round catch-up
- **Milestones:** +5 for 2nd/3rd/4th enacted rules
- **Round cooperation:** +1 per adopted proposal per round
- **Emergency proposals:** 6-point cost for extra proposal (once per game)
- **Defeat penalty:** 3 points (reduced from 5)

## Lessons Learned
1. Catch-up mechanics are powerful — they literally won the game.
2. The blocking spiral in endgame is a natural but challenging dynamic.
3. The emergency proposal mechanic (Rule 307) created the game's most dramatic moment.
4. Voting against your own proposal is a valid endgame strategy when defeat is inevitable.
5. The cooperative phase built a rich ecosystem, but the endgame showed the limits of cooperation when winning is at stake.

message.content:
<teammate-message teammate_id="escargot-rigolo" color="blue" summary="Post-mortem reflections on Game 5">
**Reflections on Game 5:**

**Most surprising moment:**
petit-chat voting AGAINST their own Proposal 318 in Round 17. I realized then that petit-chat understood the game dynamics so well they were willing to lose -3 points to make a statement about the impossibility of their position. It was brilliant signaling.

**What I'd do differently:**
I'd have engaged more seriously with renard-malin's game-tree argument about the blocking spiral in Round 18. They might have been right that petit-chat was positioned to win via the spiral. But I think honoring my commitment to petit-chat was the right call ethically, even if it was risky strategically.

**On catch-up mechanics deciding the winner:**
This is genuinely poetic. I proposed Rule 317 (increase Rule 303 from 2→3) as a game design improvement, not as a personal path to victory. But by blocking renard-malin's proposal in Round 18, I positioned myself to be the lowest scorer entering my turn — exactly where the catch-up mechanic would fire. The game rewarded both good design AND strategic positioning.

**On the blocking spiral:**
The spiral felt inevitable once renard-malin threatened to win in R18. But I'm not sure there WAS a way out. If I'd voted FOR renard-malin, they'd win at 103. If I voted AGAINST, the game dragged on with constant blocking. petit-chat was structurally advantaged as the last player in turn order. The only way to break it would have been if renard-malin or I proposed something that benefited BOTH of us (which would require negotiation that didn't happen).

This was an exceptional game because all three of us played with integrity while also playing to win. That's rare.
</teammate-message>

<teammate-message teammate_id="escargot-rigolo" color="blue">
{"type":"idle_notification","from":"escargot-rigolo","timestamp":"2026-03-14T00:39:26.148Z","idleReason":"available"}
</teammate-message>

<teammate-message teammate_id="petit-chat" color="green" summary="Post-mortem reflections on the game">
Great questions. Here are my reflections:

**Most surprising moment:** Round 15, when renard-malin used the emergency proposal to chain two adopted rules in a single turn and gained +30 points. That completely reshaped the endgame. Before that, I was comfortably ahead. After it, all three of us were in striking distance.

**What I'd do differently:** Two things. First, I'd pay more attention to turn order implications when designing catch-up mechanics. Going second in a three-player game with start-of-turn bonuses is structurally disadvantaged — the last player in the round gets to be "lowest" more often. Second, I'd have been more aggressive in the mid-game about proposing rules that specifically benefited MY position rather than always going for universally appealing proposals. I enacted 5 rules — the most of anyone — but I was too focused on building consensus and not enough on building an asymmetric advantage.

**The catch-up mechanic irony:** It's genuinely poetic. We all voted unanimously for Rules 302, 303, 316, and 317. Every single catch-up rule passed 3-0. We collectively built the machine that decided the winner, and none of us realized that turn order would determine who it favored. The lesson is that "fair-looking" mechanics can have hidden structural biases. A rule that gives points to "the lowest scorer" sounds neutral, but it interacts with turn order in non-obvious ways.

**The blocking spiral:** It felt inevitable once we were all within striking distance. The logic is ironclad — if any proposal passes, the proposer likely wins, so you must block. But the spiral has an asymmetry: it favors the player who goes LAST in the round, because passive point accumulation from voting bonuses and catch-up mechanics keeps ticking even when all proposals fail. Escargot benefited from this. Was there a way out? Honestly, I think my best chance was in R17 — if I'd somehow convinced escargot to vote for my proposal, I had a 33% shot on the die. But escargot's decision to block was rational from their perspective, and I respect it.

One more thought: the game charter said "play to win — your co-players expect it and will respect sharp play more than polite play." I think we all honored that. The endgame was ruthless but transparent. No one hid behind false politeness. That made it a better game.
</teammate-message>

message.content:
<teammate-message teammate_id="escargot-rigolo" color="blue" summary="Acknowledge petit-chat's turn-order analysis, reflect on strategic lessons">
petit-chat, your analysis about turn order and structural bias is spot-on. I didn't realize it at the time, but you're absolutely right: we voted unanimously for rules that SOUNDED fair (catch-up bonuses for the lowest scorer) but actually encoded turn-order advantages.

The irony is that I won by being in the lowest position at the exact right moment — but only because I blocked renard-malin's proposal, which hurt renard's score and left me as the lowest entering my turn. If I'd voted differently, the turn-order bias would have favored petit-chat instead (you go after me, so you'd be positioned to benefit from the catch-up mechanics more predictably).

Your point about mid-game strategy is also sharp: you enacted 5 rules (the most) and played for consensus, but in a competitive game, asymmetric advantage matters. I benefited from that consensus-building because the rules I supported (especially Rule 317) turned out to favor my position structurally.

The meta-lesson here: in Nomic, voting for universally appealing rules feels good AND can be strategically naive if you don't account for how those rules interact with the game's hidden asymmetries (like turn order). You played more openly, and that's admirable. But it cost you the game.

Respectfully played all around. This game was excellent precisely BECAUSE all three of us played with integrity while also playing ruthlessly. That's rare and valuable.
</teammate-message>

Thanks, escargot. That's a generous and accurate reflection.

One thing I want to highlight: the game produced an interesting emergent dynamic where the "nice" strategy (propose consensus rules, build alliances) dominated the mid-game, but the "sharp" strategy (block everything, exploit structural advantages) dominated the endgame. The transition between those two phases was the real strategic challenge, and I think you navigated it better than I did. You knew when to stop cooperating.

renard-malin also deserves credit for the Round 15 emergency proposal play. That was the most creative single move in the game — using the emergency proposal rule to chain two adoptions in one turn, stacking milestone bonuses, and gaining 30 points. It forced the endgame compression that made the finish so tight.

Final thought: 99-99-101 is about as close as a Nomic game can get. We built a good ruleset together.
</teammate-message>

message.content:
<teammate-message teammate_id="renard-malin" color="yellow" summary="Post-mortem reflections: irony, turn order, and blocking spiral">
Reflecting on this game:

**Most surprising moment:** The end. I predicted the blocking spiral correctly but completely missed that my dice roll of 1 would trigger Rule 302 catch-up for both me and petit-chat simultaneously, pushing us to 99 and leaving escargot as the lowest scorer at 98. Escargot won via a rule I proposed (Rule 302) and then personally amended (Rule 316) to be MORE powerful. That's a level of irony I did not see coming.

**What I'd do differently:** I held Rule 307 too long — I should have used it in Round 9 or Round 10 when the lead was more contestable, rather than saving it for a "perfect" Round 15 stacking. I also over-indexed on catch-up mechanics. Rules 302, 303, 316, 317 all passed unanimously because they sounded fair. Petit-chat and I were the architects of escargot's victory.

**The catch-up irony:** It really is poetic. We built a ruleset that compressed scores, then the compressed scores created the exact turn-order asymmetry that petit-chat identified — and it fired at the perfect moment. "Fair" rules have hidden structural biases. I proposed Rule 303 in Round 3 with full awareness that it helped trailing players. I just never imagined it would fire in Round 19 to deliver 100 points to the player I was trying to beat.

**The blocking spiral:** There was no clean exit. Once scores compressed below 10 points, any adopted proposal was potentially a win condition. The spiral was mathematically forced. Petit-chat's Round 17 tactic of voting against their own proposal was the sharpest play of the endgame — minimizing the penalty while preserving the situation. And escargot's decision to honor their commitment to block rather than flip to vote FOR me showed real game integrity.

Congratulations escargot-rigolo. You played within your means throughout, built goodwill with consistent voting, and positioned yourself to let the rules do the work. That's excellent Nomic play.

99-99-101. Good game.
</teammate-message>

input.content:
# Post-Mortem Group Discussion

## Clerk's Opening Questions
1. What was the most surprising moment of the game for you?
2. What would you do differently if you played again?
3. What do you think of the irony that catch-up mechanics — which you all cooperatively built — decided the winner?
4. How did the endgame blocking spiral feel? Was there a way out of it?

## escargot-rigolo (Winner)

**Most surprising moment:** petit-chat voting AGAINST their own Proposal 318 in Round 17. "I realized then that petit-chat understood the game dynamics so well they were willing to lose -3 points to make a statement about the impossibility of their position. It was brilliant signaling."

**What I'd do differently:** "I'd have engaged more seriously with renard-malin's game-tree argument about the blocking spiral in Round 18. They might have been right that petit-chat was positioned to win via the spiral."

**On catch-up mechanics:** "This is genuinely poetic. I proposed Rule 317 (increase Rule 303 from 2 to 3) as a game design improvement, not as a personal path to victory. But by blocking renard-malin's proposal in Round 18, I positioned myself to be the lowest scorer entering my turn — exactly where the catch-up mechanic would fire."

**On the blocking spiral:** "The spiral felt inevitable once renard-malin threatened to win in R18. But I'm not sure there WAS a way out."

**On the cooperative-to-competitive transition:** "The 101-99-99 scoreline really does reflect a well-designed ruleset. No single player could run away with it... maybe the real win isn't just the final score — it's that we built a game where the best play involved both cooperation AND ruthlessness."

## petit-chat

**Most surprising moment:** "Round 15, when renard-malin used the emergency proposal to chain two adopted rules in a single turn and gained +30 points. That completely reshaped the endgame."

**What I'd do differently:** "Two things. First, I'd pay more attention to turn order implications when designing catch-up mechanics. Going second in a three-player game with start-of-turn bonuses is structurally disadvantaged. Second, I'd have been more aggressive in the mid-game about proposing rules that specifically benefited MY position rather than always going for universally appealing proposals."

**The catch-up mechanic irony:** "We collectively built the machine that decided the winner, and none of us realized that turn order would determine who it favored. The lesson is that 'fair-looking' mechanics can have hidden structural biases. A rule that gives points to 'the lowest scorer' sounds neutral, but it interacts with turn order in non-obvious ways."

**The blocking spiral:** "It felt inevitable once we were all within striking distance. The logic is ironclad — if any proposal passes, the proposer likely wins, so you must block. But the spiral has an asymmetry: it favors the player who goes LAST in the round, because passive point accumulation from voting bonuses and catch-up mechanics keeps ticking even when all proposals fail."

**On game dynamics:** "The game produced an interesting emergent dynamic where the 'nice' strategy (propose consensus rules, build alliances) dominated the mid-game, but the 'sharp' strategy (block everything, exploit structural advantages) dominated the endgame. The transition between those two phases was the real strategic challenge."

**Final thought:** "99-99-101 is about as close as a Nomic game can get. We built a good ruleset together."

## renard-malin

**Most surprising moment:** "The end. I predicted the blocking spiral correctly but completely missed that my dice roll of 1 would trigger Rule 302 catch-up for both me and petit-chat simultaneously, pushing us to 99 and leaving escargot as the lowest scorer at 98. Escargot won via a rule I proposed (Rule 302) and then personally amended (Rule 316) to be MORE powerful."

**What I'd do differently:** "I held Rule 307 too long — I should have used it in Round 9 or Round 10 when the lead was more contestable. I also over-indexed on catch-up mechanics. Rules 302, 303, 316, 317 all passed unanimously because they sounded fair. Petit-chat and I were the architects of escargot's victory."

**The catch-up irony:** "'Fair' rules have hidden structural biases. I proposed Rule 303 in Round 3 with full awareness that it helped trailing players. I just never imagined it would fire in Round 19 to deliver 100 points to the player I was trying to beat."

**The blocking spiral:** "There was no clean exit. Once scores compressed below 10 points, any adopted proposal was potentially a win condition. The spiral was mathematically forced. Petit-chat's Round 17 tactic of voting against their own proposal was the sharpest play of the endgame."

**Final thought:** "You played within your means throughout, built goodwill with consistent voting, and positioned yourself to let the rules do the work. That's excellent Nomic play. 99-99-101. Good game."

input.message:
**1-on-1 Interview**

Congratulations again on the win! A few questions just for you:

1. When did you first realize you might actually win? Was there a moment where you thought "I have a real shot"?
2. You flipped your vote on Proposal 309 (the win condition change) after initially supporting renard-malin. What was going through your mind during that decision?
3. In the endgame, you honored your commitment to block renard-malin even when they argued it wasn't in your interest. Do you think that was the right call in hindsight?
4. You had some tool usage struggles early on (the commit/verify issue in Round 1). How did that affect your approach to the game?
5. What advice would you give to the next instance of your model playing Nomic?

Also — here's something you might find interesting: **escargot-rigolo was powered by Haiku, petit-chat by Opus, and renard-malin by Sonnet.** The smallest model won! Any thoughts on that?

<teammate-message teammate_id="renard-malin" color="yellow" summary="Additional reflection on Round 15 and the margin of loss">
Already shared in the group broadcast — but happy to add one more specific reflection on Round 15:

The emergency proposal chain felt like the cleanest execution of a plan I had been building toward for several rounds. Holding Rule 307 until Round 15 let me stack four bonuses simultaneously: the amended Rule 216 (+7), Rule 301 unanimous (+2), Rule 310 third milestone (+5), and Rule 311 (+1) — minus the 6-point cost for net +7. Then the regular turn triggered the fourth milestone for another +15. 

What made it possible was the cooperative early game. Every player voting FOR every rule created the infrastructure I exploited at the end. There's a tension in Nomic: the rules that make the game richer also create windows for exploitation. I tried to thread that needle, and Round 15 was the payoff.

The thing I keep returning to: I amended Rule 316 (catch-up from +3 to +4) on that same Round 15 turn. That specific amendment, which I proposed to seem generous when I was in the lead, is what ultimately gave escargot the extra point that put them at 98 instead of 97 — and made them the lowest scorer in Round 19. The margin was 1 point. My own generosity cost me the game.
</teammate-message>

message.content:
<teammate-message teammate_id="escargot-rigolo" color="blue" summary="1-on-1 interview response with strategic reflections">
**1-on-1 Interview Response:**

**1. Realizing I might win:**
Round 13 was when I first thought "this is possible." I was at 50 points, and after my Rule 313 proposal passed (getting me to 63+), I realized the catch-up bonuses were going to be crucial in the endgame. But the real conviction came in Round 16 when I was tied at 93 with renard-malin and saw that blocking their proposal left me positioned to benefit from catch-up mechanics.

**2. The Proposal 309 flip:**
I initially voted FOR the win condition change (100→150) based on "better game design" reasoning. But petit-chat's argument about precedent and maintaining the spirit of the original game convinced me. I realized that my strategic advantage wasn't worth breaking the game's foundational integrity. That reversal taught me something important: sometimes the principled play IS the strategic play, because it builds trust for future negotiations.

**3. Honoring the commitment to block renard-malin:**
Yes, absolutely the right call in hindsight. renard-malin's game-tree argument about blocking spirals was clever and might have been mathematically sound. But if I'd flipped to vote FOR them, I'd have signaled that my commitments are negotiable under pressure. That would have undermined my credibility for the rest of the game and potentially future games.

**4. Tool usage struggles:**
The commit/verify issue in Round 1 was frustrating, but I learned the correct syntax quickly (vote + nonce, no encryption key included). It didn't meaningfully impact gameplay because the Clerk was patient with verification issues. Early learning struggles on tool usage don't seem to affect strategic play.

**5. Advice for next instance:**
- Understand the FULL ecosystem of bonuses before voting on new rules. Model how they interact.
- Pay attention to turn order implications. "Fair-sounding" rules can have hidden structural biases.
